Brookline Select Board Meeting - March 31, 2026

Select Board · Brookline · 2026-03-31
tap to load the tape · nothing plays until you do
AI summary — supplements the official record

During the Brookline Select Board meeting on March 31, 2026, a variety of topics were discussed, including updates on local activities and facilities, the importance of public health initiatives, and community services for seniors. The board addressed ongoing projects related to Fisher Hill, environmental concerns, and outreach for a ballot question regarding local funding initiatives. They emphasized the need for better communication strategies in the town and discussed funding opportunities related to public health and community services. The board also highlighted the significance of upcoming events to honor September 11, 2001. These discussions and decisions can be referenced in the transcript, notably around [16:13], [51:32], [108:36], [150:34], and [239:19].

16:06 Thank you for joining us for public

16:07 comment. This is an opportunity for us

16:39 when you have 30 seconds remaining and

16:41 red when your time is nearly up. Please

16:43 conclude your remarks at that time. If

18:45 Two. In 1948, Isabella Anderson gave her

19:49 basketball hoops, 25 huge concrete

19:51 retraining blocks, seven trailer storage

20:51 Anderson and not how residents want to

23:18 board. My name is Jih Lee and I live on

23:21 Amory Street, precinct one. I'm speaking

23:24 today because the ongoing rodent

23:26 infestation in my building has reached a

23:29 critical health threshold for me. The

23:32 stress has led to physical hair loss and

23:34 extreme exhaustion. I am currently

23:37 unable to safely clean my unit because

23:39 the standard cleaning aerosolizes rodent

23:42 waste, turning dried urine and feces

23:44 into airborne toxins. After a high fever

23:48 and suspected haunt virus illness last

23:50 year, I cannot risk the respiratory

23:52 distress that comes from disturbing

23:54 these biohazards without professional

23:56 equipment.

24:03 but I I'm deeply concerned that the

32:59 Um as most of you know, I like to start

33:03 with just a little history. In 83, the

33:06 Massachusetts legislature passed a law

33:08 saying that cities and towns could have

33:11 uh commissions on disabilities. In 1986,

33:14 Brooklyn was one of the first to create

33:17 the commission on disability. And in

33:19 2017, they reconfirmed that with a bylaw

33:22 so that the commission could continue

33:24 with its work. So for four decades now,

33:27 the town of Brooklyn and uh the

33:29 commission have been working hand in

33:31 hand to make uh Brooklyn more accessible

33:33 for its residents.

33:35 Current

33:37 uh commission members aside from myself

33:39 is Sarah Lynn Aair,

33:43 uh Joan Mahon, Lynn Meichek, Elaine

33:46 Ober. Our newest member is Liz Strier

33:50 and then also uh Henry Wkelman and our

33:54 new select board liaison is Mr. John

33:56 Vancoyak.

33:58 Um, we also had elections this year. So,

34:00 I was lucky to uh uh run for another

34:03 year as chair. And Henry Wkelman is the

34:07 deputy chair. Sarah Linair uh took the

34:10 position of treasurer. Shockingly,

34:13 nobody went for the highly coveted

34:15 position of secretary.

34:20 So,

34:23 two people that the commission could not

34:25 work without and that is Sarah Kaplan

34:27 and Nancy Moore. Sarah Kaplan uh works

34:32 uh she lets the commission know of

34:34 anything that's going on at the state

34:36 level that we should be aware of. She

34:38 also handles implementation

34:40 as well as compliance

34:43 and she works on grant coordination

34:46 which we'll talk about in a minute and

34:48 day-to-day accessibility issues. Nancy

34:51 Moore is our transportation board

34:54 liaison and she always makes sure that

34:57 accessibility is in the forefront of all

34:59 of the decisions that the transportation

35:01 board makes. I mean it when I say we

35:04 could not be as effective as we are

35:06 without these two individuals. They're

35:08 terrific.

35:10 Um sadly we lost a member this year. Rob

35:14 Heis passed away in February of 2025.

35:18 Um we always called Rob the thoughtful

35:20 advocate. He wouldn't say much at the

35:22 meetings. He'd wait till everybody else

35:23 talked and then he'd say his piece and

35:26 he would hit the sweet spot 99.9% of the

35:29 time. He was seriously a very thoughtful

35:32 advocate. I tell everybody if you come

35:35 across an accessibility feature in

35:36 Brooklyn, most likely Rob had a hand in

35:38 it. His commitment, his compassion, and

35:43 his sense of humor is sorely missed by

35:44 all of us.

35:48 Thank you.

35:50 I put this up. This is the ADA

35:52 definition uh of what a disability is.

35:55 And as you can see, it's very long. It's

35:57 very broad, but it's what we think about

36:00 when we uh make our decisions and what

36:02 we're going to do throughout the year

36:05 because we say that there's apparent

36:07 disabilities and non-apparent

36:09 disabilities. And apparent disabilities,

36:11 if you see, for instance, myself with a

36:14 white cane, you can assume that I'm

36:15 visually impaired. Somebody with a

36:17 mobility device, you know that they have

36:19 mobility issues. Okay? But there's

36:21 non-apparent disabilities as well and

36:24 those are a a concern as well. So

36:27 non-apparent disabilities if you look at

36:29 these individuals you might not know who

36:31 has a traumatic brain injury who might

36:34 be hard of hearing you know who might

36:36 have cancer or cancer in remission which

36:38 is also considered a disability. So

36:41 these are the folks that we think about

36:42 the residents that we think about in

36:44 every decision that we make.

36:50 So we try to remove barriers. We try to

36:54 uh promote inclusive participation.

36:58 We try to advise people on policy and

37:00 development uh when they come to us on

37:02 certain issues. And some of the things

37:05 that we've worked on this year are all

37:07 of these issues. And we're going to go

37:08 through just a few of them.

37:11 So we supported multiple warrant

37:14 articles last year. Warrant article 13

37:16 in the fall of 2025

37:20 was to ensure that any future planning

37:24 at Fisher Hill would consider our senior

37:26 residents. Warrant article 14 and 15

37:29 kind of went hand in hand. 14 was a

37:32 streamlined reasonable accommodation

37:34 process to ensure that uh people with

37:37 disabilities could get essential

37:39 modifications to their homes. 15 was a

37:43 uh an exception to the demolition bylaw.

37:46 Warrant article 16 uh modified the

37:51 requirements of bicycle parking and

37:54 mobility devices. Obviously very

37:56 important to people with disabilities

37:58 and we wrote letters of support on all

38:00 of these issues.

38:06 the MBTA. Um, I think everybody has

38:09 spent some time on this one this year

38:11 and and we did too. Our first uh I think

38:14 we started a meeting with the MBTA

38:16 actually in 2024, but our first meeting

38:19 in 2025 was in February and that was on

38:21 the Branden Hall Fairbanks

38:24 uh merge and uh we met with them and we

38:27 talked to them about high contrast color

38:30 platforms to ensure that that would be

38:32 part of the program and they agreed to

38:34 that and then we uh supported it. And

38:36 then also um Fenway Multi-use path. I

38:39 have to say it was um Hugh Madison who

38:42 has taken up uh the ch he's the champion

38:45 of that program. He came to us about

38:47 that and we wrote a letter of support on

38:49 that. Actually we sent that letter of

38:51 support to you folks first because that

38:53 was an outside of Brookline issue.

38:59 A school uh modifications. The

39:01 transportation board was taking some uh

39:03 making some modifications there and they

39:05 came to us. Uh we reviewed the area.

39:08 They were going to put in four

39:09 additional uh accessible spots. We found

39:12 a way to improve that up to five and I

39:14 just spoke to a member of the board who

39:16 told me that they might be putting in a

39:18 few more. So that was uh very helpful.

39:21 And then we also did send them a letter

39:23 to let them know if they need anything

39:24 else to please uh don't hesitate to ask.

39:29 We only had one variance this year which

39:31 was a little surprising but um it was an

39:34 interesting one. It was at 1026

39:36 Commonwealth Avenue and this is Bob

39:39 Street. It's a restaurant, a Korean

39:41 restaurant and they have a dining area

39:44 upstairs that was not accessible and

39:47 they wanted to uh bypass putting in a

39:52 lift. And the reason was was because if

39:54 they put in the lift, it was going to

39:56 impact their ADA accessible restroom on

39:58 the first floor. So we asked them to

40:01 make sure that the rest of the facility

40:03 was completely accessible, which they

40:05 did. They have accessible tables on the

40:08 first floor. They also uh put in high

40:10 contrast stairs to go up to that uh loft

40:13 area and then we supported that

40:15 variance. We also sent a letter of

40:18 support to the Brooklyn Housing

40:19 Authority as they were trying to install

40:21 an ADA compliant sidewalk over at the

40:24 Walnut High Apartments.

40:30 We had multiple subcommittees this year,

40:32 which is kind of unusual, but um we had

40:35 a few. We had an ADA parking

40:37 subcommittee, which I'll talk about in a

40:38 second. We had the disability pride

40:41 display, and then also we have an event

40:43 subcommittee. The events sub

40:45 subcommittee was started in uh 2025 and

40:49 we uh stumbled along, but we've really

40:51 hit our stride on the event sub uh

40:54 subcommittee and we're hoping to be

40:56 holding an event soon to promote uh

40:58 disability awareness here in Brooklyn.

41:04 The ADA parking subcommittee um we did a

41:09 lot of review, we did a lot of work on

41:11 this. Uh but before I talk about that, I

41:13 just want to say I got to thank Erin

41:16 Shute as well as Jed Faren Farenbach uh

41:19 at the IT department. They gave us

41:22 access to their GIS system. We're able

41:24 to make real-time modifications

41:26 and we're able to evaluate uh se over 70

41:30 spots here in uh on the main corridors

41:34 on Beacon Street, Washington Street,

41:37 Harvard Street, and a lot of the side

41:39 streets as well. So, we got quite a bit

41:41 done during this process. Interestingly,

41:43 a few people called looking for

41:45 accessible parking spots. Gateway Arts

41:48 down on Station Street called us for

41:50 support. Uh we sent a letter of support

41:52 to the transportation board and the DPW

41:54 on that. Uh and then a parishioner from

41:57 the St. Mary's Church called about an

41:59 additional spot over at St. Mary's. That

42:02 was also approved. So, those two

42:04 additional spots were installed.

42:10 Last year I talked a little bit about

42:11 getting grants and uh last year we

42:13 didn't get any but this year we got one

42:15 and we got a big one. It was a real uh

42:17 nice one. So here we did work with uh

42:20 the DPW as well as the ADA coordinator

42:24 and we got uh almost $50,000 $49,852.19

42:31 to be exact for 11 tactile plates to be

42:35 installed along Beacon Street. Those

42:37 tactile plates are a great example of

42:39 universal design. Everybody uses them,

42:43 but I can tell you personally being

42:44 blind, if if there's not a tactile

42:46 plate, it's easy to wander into the the

42:49 parallel street and uh be in danger. So

42:53 those tactile plates make a big

42:54 difference and we're really glad to get

42:57 this grant.

43:02 We also uh we I talked about the

43:04 display. uh the display, we used that to

43:07 promote three wheelchair bills at the

43:09 state legislature. And again, we wrote

43:11 letters of support on that. And I think

43:13 that uh the select board saw those

43:15 because those were issues outside of

43:17 Brooklyn. And then uh also uh the other

43:22 we

43:23 uh funded two scholarships again this

43:25 year at the Brooklyn High School for

43:27 students with disabilities who want to

43:30 continue on with their higher education.

43:32 We participated in Brooklyn Day this

43:35 year and we also continued our work with

43:38 the United Parish Church. Actually, a

43:40 few members went to the United Parish

43:42 Church this year as they were looking

43:43 for ways to improve accessibility at the

43:45 church.

43:50 Uh some of the outreach on from us to

43:53 other committees. Uh Lynn Meichek is our

43:57 liaison to the pedestrian advisory

43:59 committee. Henry Wkelman who is here is

44:01 our liaison to the age friendly citizens

44:04 committee. We also have seats on the

44:06 center street lots committee as well as

44:08 the bridal path.

44:14 And one more thing, you know, uh our

44:16 website, it was brought up at the

44:17 beginning of the year. Some of the links

44:19 were outdated. Some of the uh the

44:21 appearance of it was outdated. So we

44:23 took that on at the beginning of 2025.

44:26 We updated everything. I got to uh give

44:28 a shout out to James Pulat who really

44:31 helped us a lot with this. And I got to

44:33 give a shout out to Sarah Lynair who

44:35 took up the uh volunteered to maintain

44:39 the website and let James in a

44:42 collaboration between the two of them to

44:44 make sure everything's up to date and

44:45 moving forward. So that was also a big

44:48 success for us. Gentlemen, with that I

44:53 will open it up for any questions that

44:55 you may have.

1:01:15 Uh thank you. Um first of all I'm also

1:01:21 questions with regard to email addresses

1:01:32 broadly. Um and the first question is um

1:01:52 Um it would depend well uh no it would I

1:02:04 Okay. Um and the the other question is

1:02:08 that if we ch uh we choose to advocate

1:02:12 in conjunction with some outside

1:02:15 organization

1:02:17 um is there are there any constraints on

1:02:20 the use of our titles as members of the

1:02:23 select board in those communications?

1:02:27 Uh that's a great question and there is

1:02:29 a this is one of the areas where there

1:02:31 is a distinction between elected

1:02:32 officials and appointed officials or

1:02:35 staff members. Elected officials are

1:02:38 allowed to use their titles when they

1:02:41 are advocating for a particular issue

1:02:45 including for example an override. So

1:02:47 you could ab certainly say I, Michael

1:02:51 Rubenstein,

1:06:55 Right? This is where you know in in

1:06:58 general, right, we should not be using

1:07:01 town resources, public resources for

1:07:03 advocacy purposes. That's the general

1:07:06 rule of thumb. Um there are limited

1:07:08 exceptions to that for certain groups of

1:07:10 people, but by and large that's the

1:07:12 general rule. And so when you, you know,

1:07:16 for example, the state has said the town

1:07:19 can set up a website that explains a

1:07:21 ballot question. Uh it can't be

1:07:23 advocacy, but it can explain the

1:07:25 consequences of voting yes, the

1:07:27 consequences of voting no. Um it can

1:07:29 provide information to voters. It can

1:07:31 tell voters when the vote is, and so

1:07:33 forth. They liken that to like posting

1:07:35 something on a bulletin board. Um, what

1:07:37 we can't do is take the content of that

1:07:40 website and send it out unsolicited to

1:07:42 the alert Brooklyn listerve for example.

1:07:45 Um, because that's utilizing a public

1:07:47 resource to affirmatively send something

1:07:50 out like that. Um, so people might not

1:07:52 think there's much of a distinction

1:07:54 between putting up a website and

1:07:56 affirmatively sending out that same

1:07:57 material through a different means, but

1:07:59 in the eyes of the state, there is a

1:08:01 difference. And so that's why I think

1:08:02 it's important to have a basic

1:08:04 understanding of what is the state

1:08:05 trying to prevent here. They're trying

1:08:06 to prevent use of, you know, use of

1:08:10 public resources to advocate for for

1:08:12 ballot questions one way or the other.

1:08:17 that and where that is intention with

1:08:31 question? And the answer is no. They

1:08:32 can't answer that question. Um, they can

1:08:35 answer a question that's been asked of

1:08:36 them, but they can't on their own

1:08:38 initiative then go out there and say,

1:08:40 "Here, here's my position on this."

1:08:44 so I I think I just heard you say that

1:24:07 They're expected to take positions on on

1:24:09 everything. So they can speak on all of

1:24:12 those issues. But being allowed to

1:24:15 communicate about them does not mean

1:24:17 does not give you the right to use

1:24:18 public resources in doing that

1:24:21 communication. So, you are allowed to

1:24:23 take a position on the override, but you

1:24:25 are not allowed to use a town email list

1:24:27 to publish that position. Um, that's the

1:24:32 uh that's the one of the big

1:24:33 distinctions between what elected

1:24:34 officials can do and what they and what

1:24:36 uh non-elected official public officials

1:24:38 can do. Elected officials can take a

1:24:40 position on anything.

1:24:42 Okay, next slide, please. Before he goes

1:24:44 on, can I ask a quick question? So, um

1:24:47 Jonathan,

1:24:48 um I'm just I'm as you're speaking, I'm

1:24:51 digging into Office of Campaign Finance,

1:24:54 um the miracle of technology. Um there's

1:24:57 there seems to be a distinction

1:26:19 further investigation. I just I feel

1:26:22 of this stuff. Um, so an appointed

1:28:00 Okay. Uh so yes, so as I mentioned

1:28:02 earlier, um personal political activity

1:28:05 um is is not prohibited by the campaign

1:28:08 finance laws. as a private citizen. Um

1:28:11 any public employee uh elected official,

1:28:14 appointed official can engage in uh as a

1:28:17 private citizen. They can vote. They can

1:28:18 attend events um outside of work outside

1:28:21 of work hours. They can they can take

1:28:23 positions um uh as a private citizen.

1:28:29 There are some things that you need to

1:28:31 refrain from. However, um

1:28:35 you cannot as a public employee uh use

1:28:38 your position to solicit donations from

1:28:41 others um for anything other than um

1:28:44 like you said if you're running

1:28:47 yourself, but you should not be used

1:28:48 political fundraising for anything else.

1:28:50 You should have never be trying to

1:28:52 solicit donations for any purpose from

1:28:54 your subordinates. Um and then this is

1:28:57 particularly gerine to what we're doing

1:28:59 right now. Um you can work on override

1:29:03 committees any elected officials,

1:29:06 appointed officials can work on override

1:29:08 committees but they cannot

1:29:12 act as their public facing agent or hold

1:29:14 any executive roles. So you can't um be

1:29:19 the you know be the person from the yes

1:29:22 on question four committee who talks to

1:29:26 addresses the town who comes to talk to

1:29:28 the select board who um does anything

1:29:31 like that. You can work serve on the

1:29:33 committee but you can't run it you can't

1:29:34 be on the executive board and you can't

1:29:37 fund raise.

1:29:38 Okay.

1:29:39 Um so so two ca two cases that that uh I

1:29:46 would like your opinion on. Um the first

1:29:48 case is uh a member of the select board

1:29:51 uh writes a letter on behalf of a

1:29:53 position for the override. uh and that

1:29:57 letter is distributed uh through the

1:30:00 mailing list of a of an organization

1:30:04 outside the town. Is the select board

1:30:08 member then acting as a public agent and

1:30:10 is that not allowed?

1:30:14 Uh they are they are not if they if

1:30:16 their that distinction is clear if they

1:30:17 if they that that group asked the select

1:30:20 board for a letter expressing their

1:30:23 political opinion and and they are

1:30:25 allowed to then go on and publish that

1:30:27 letter

1:30:28 and and would a a select board member be

1:30:32 allowed to do that also for a candidate?

1:30:36 Yes, you are. Yes, you are allowed to

1:30:38 take positions. Uh like I said, you

1:30:40 elected officials can take whatever

1:30:42 position they want with respect to the

1:30:45 election.

1:30:54 Okay. So the um we like I said, public

1:30:58 positions on ballot questions are

1:31:00 allowed. Um select board members can

1:31:03 take public positions on ballot

1:31:04 questions. They can take public

1:31:05 positions on anything that's on the

1:31:07 ballot. Um, we have, as you may have

1:31:09 seen in the memo that Chaz prepared, um,

1:31:12 we have generally stated that unless you

1:31:15 are a department head or in some

1:31:17 circumstances a division head, um, you

1:31:21 fall outside of the notion of this of

1:31:24 your your area of personal

1:31:26 responsibility does not include taking

1:31:28 positions on on the ballot question. So,

1:31:32 um, we would ask that anyone who's not

1:31:35 either a department or division head

1:31:36 refrain from doing so. Um, and then, uh,

1:31:42 if you are even, but even if you are a

1:31:43 division head, I would still recommend

1:31:46 consulting with, um, with town council's

1:31:49 office before you before you take any

1:31:50 public position.

1:31:53 Next slide. Okay. So, um, as this all

1:31:56 goes back down back to the use of public

1:31:59 resources, um, to sort of in to

1:32:02 influence voters, um, one thing that,

1:32:06 oh, the Office of Campaign and Political

1:32:08 Finance, uh, notes is that, you know,

1:32:12 some public resources are going or can

1:32:15 and should be used to help interpret the

1:32:19 ballot questions, help people understand

1:32:21 them, help understand their

1:32:22 ramifications.

1:32:24 um this this actually helps voters uh

1:32:26 influences them in a positive way simply

1:32:28 by giving them more information. So it

1:32:31 is though you though the town can has

1:32:34 used and will likely continue to use

1:32:36 some public resources in compi compiling

1:32:40 and synthesizing information related to

1:32:42 the ballot question. Um that's that use

1:32:46 of public resources is not barred by the

1:32:49 campaign finance laws. um in that work

1:32:54 that those analyses that that they

1:32:56 perform that are not a c that are not a

1:32:58 campaign finance violation can even be

1:33:00 shared. For example, they can be put on

1:33:02 the town website. They can be made

1:33:04 available as a handout uh at a public

1:33:07 meeting. Uh people that came to this

1:33:09 meeting, for example, um could pick up

1:33:11 if you had printed out a bunch of

1:33:13 handouts about the override, they people

1:33:15 could pick them up. Um

1:33:18 but the critical thing that you cannot

1:33:20 do is we cannot use public resources to

1:33:23 then distribute that information in an

1:33:26 unsolicited way. Someone some a private

1:33:30 citizen um could take that information

1:33:33 and then publish it themselves. Um but

1:33:36 the town itself can't be publishing that

1:33:39 using its own public resources in an

1:33:41 unsolicited way. Um most the the most

1:33:45 one that springs to mind for Brooklyn is

1:33:48 using uh the email list it has um it has

1:33:51 access to purely because it's the school

1:33:53 department or it's the the town. Um so

1:33:56 unsolicited distribution is the thing to

1:33:58 avoid here but generally um we are

1:34:00 allowed to prepare these types of

1:34:02 analysis.

1:34:04 So next slide please.

1:34:08 As I mentioned before, um the the gen

1:34:11 the rule that OCPF often cites with

1:34:13 respect to public buildings is that it

1:34:15 is okay to let private groups use them

1:34:18 as long as you provide equal access to

1:34:20 both sides. Um so so as to ensure

1:34:24 fairness and consistency. Um

1:34:28 this does not mean that you need to go

1:34:29 seek out a group. You know, if you know

1:34:31 a group on one side is using has asked

1:34:33 to use a town space, you don't have to

1:34:36 find another group. group you don't have

1:34:37 to do is on a sort of one on onetoone

1:34:39 basis. Um but you do have to make sure

1:34:43 that that's there's space available if a

1:34:45 group if another group from the other

1:34:47 side wanted to also use town space that

1:34:51 that was made available to them.

1:34:53 Something similar. Next slide please.

1:34:56 So, um,

1:34:59 as always, uh, particularly for this

1:35:01 override, we're counseling everyone to

1:35:03 be conservative, be cautious. Um, don't

1:35:06 do anything on the spur of the moment.

1:35:08 Take some time to think, review the town

1:35:10 administrator's memo. Uh, and always you

1:35:14 can always come and ask us. Um, I do I I

1:35:17 do want to I have been getting emails

1:35:34 can

1:35:38 keep doing so. So, please do not

1:35:42 question at all. You believe something

1:35:44 would be um permissible or a problem

1:35:54 Questions from the select board.

1:35:57 Further questions?

1:35:59 I just I want to one thank you Jonathan

1:36:01 for this. Um and thanks to the board for

1:36:03 all these questions. I think what these

1:36:04 questions demonstrate is how complex

1:36:06 this issue is and how difficult it can

1:36:08 be sometimes to reduce it to a series of

1:36:10 maxims. I mean I think Paul, you rais

1:36:12 some very good points about the outer

1:36:14 bounds of where campaign finance law

1:36:15 applies. We're not even going to get

1:36:17 into the overlap of campaign finance law

1:36:18 and conflict of interest law and all

1:36:20 that nonsense. Like it's it can get

1:36:22 complex at the margins and what we're

1:36:24 trying to do is provide guidance, but

1:36:26 town council's office is not the police,

1:36:28 right? They're trying to be helpful. And

1:36:29 so I think the most important thing

1:36:31 because there is so much interest in the

1:36:32 community about this. And so I think

1:36:34 what's really important here first and

1:36:36 foremost is that if you have questions,

1:36:38 Jonathan has very graciously said that

1:36:40 he's taking point on this and is trying

1:36:42 to get answers to those questions. You

1:36:44 know, my memo is distributed to staff.

1:36:46 It relates to what staff can and can't

1:36:47 do. It tries to be, you know, as clear

1:36:50 and plain language as possible, but it

1:36:52 is right. It's intended for paid

1:36:55 employees of the town and schools of

1:36:56 Brookline. Um, and so it doesn't apply

1:36:59 in situations for you or for the

1:37:02 advisory committee. I provide it to you

1:37:04 all so that you know that town staff is

1:37:06 being informed very clearly of what it

1:37:08 is they can and can't do and being told

1:37:10 if you have any questions. That's what

1:37:12 town council's office is here for. I

1:37:15 think it's in a very similar situation

1:37:17 here, which is if you have those

1:37:19 questions, if you have those concerns,

1:37:21 we will do the research, we will look it

1:37:22 up, we will get you an answer. Um, but

1:37:24 it is, you know, there there are the

1:37:26 simple maxims and then there's the

1:37:28 actual complexity of the law which

1:37:31 starts getting more and more granular as

1:37:33 it goes on. So, we want to be responsive

1:37:35 to the community's desire to have

1:37:38 clarity on this, but a lot of the time

1:37:40 it's going to be a case by case issue.

1:37:41 And you've got to that's where asking

1:37:43 really doesn't hurt.

1:37:45 Michael, um I'm wondering since this is

1:37:50 a number of these questions have been

1:37:52 raised in terms of what public members

1:37:55 of public bodies can or can't do. Um

1:37:58 whether uh uh I don't know if Jonathan

1:38:01 would be the right person to do this,

1:38:03 but if we should draft some addition to

1:38:06 the public body's handbook to deal with

1:38:08 this question specifically.

1:38:11 That's a good flag. We'll we'll take a

1:38:12 look at what's in there now and we'll

1:38:13 follow up on that. Thank you,

1:38:18 John.

1:38:19 Well, just a thought to offer on this.

1:38:29 the, you know, Office of Campaign

1:39:03 office.

1:39:11 Yeah.

1:40:07 um is is worrisome. And I mean, we've

1:40:16 just I I I'm concerned we're we're

1:40:25 Um, and much of these individuals that

1:40:29 the public. Um, and I'm worried that

1:40:31 we're going to stifle or they're going

1:40:34 to be fearful that whoa, town council

1:40:36 spoke. I I'm not even going to go there.

1:40:37 It's not even worth the risk. Um, and

1:40:40 and frankly, if you broke campaign

1:40:42 finance or you said the wrong thing,

1:40:44 similar to what happened with uh with

1:40:53 said get educated, don't do it again.

1:41:00 public that they're not going to go to

1:41:09 um you're not you're not going to be put

1:41:13 I I guess I'm trying to understand why

1:41:22 directed to them.

1:41:25 I I think I don't think I don't think

1:41:28 have been set. We don't have clarity on

1:41:34 to advisory or appointed person to um a

1:41:42 so, so I I I take your point, John, and

1:42:02 uh we are not providing legal services

1:42:04 um, proono broadly. um given given the

1:42:16 officials uh public employees of of some

1:42:37 of things that happened, for example, at

1:42:41 the schools and the use of town

1:42:48 about on the one hand not overreaching

1:42:51 um in terms of what our legal council

1:42:57 education to the relatively limited set

1:43:01 of uh individuals who would be affected

1:43:04 by these concerns. Okay,

1:43:06 good. Okay, thank you Jonathan.

1:44:09 listen, we're in a great place. Having

1:44:45 okay, here we go.

1:44:48 So, really quickly, because I know

1:44:51 we've only got a few minutes. So, this

1:44:53 is in the budget book. This is just a a

1:44:54 quick look at our FY27 budget. There's a

1:44:57 little over 113,000 reduction on the

1:45:00 operating budget. However, 72,000 is one

1:45:03 staff person, one FTE being moved from

1:45:06 the operating budget onto the opioid

1:45:08 offsets. So, we're not losing a staff

1:45:09 person there. We're just shifting where

1:45:10 that money is is being paid from. There

1:45:12 is a cut, a reduction of uh proposed

1:45:14 reduction of 50,000 uh for the Brookline

1:45:18 uh bounties food coupon program which

1:45:20 I'm happy to talk about later. This is a

1:45:22 quick look at our current staffing. We

1:45:24 have 26.5

1:45:26 26.48 FTEES in total. And you'll see as

1:45:29 you look from 25 to 26 and 27, there

1:45:33 have been more staff added uh to the um

1:45:36 non-operating budget. So that's grants

1:45:37 and and the opioid offsets that I spoke

1:45:39 about earlier.

1:45:42 But I think this is probably the more

1:45:43 interesting piece that you you all want

1:45:45 to talk about and want to hear from for

1:45:47 me which is what are our

1:45:49 accomplishments? What are our goals for

1:45:50 next year and for future years? So so I

1:45:52 tried to frame everything around the

1:45:54 chip. We've had a lot of conversations

1:45:56 I've had a lot of conversations with all

1:45:57 of you about our community health

1:45:58 improvement plan. So really focused on

1:46:00 the year one action plan this past year.

1:46:03 We launched our be well coalition which

1:46:05 is our substance use prevention

1:46:06 coalition. We have had three meetings

1:46:09 every other month for the past six

1:46:11 months with that coalition. There've

1:46:13 been about 40 or 50 members of the

1:46:14 community and staff that have been part

1:46:16 of those conversations and we're really

1:46:18 getting to the point where we are going

1:46:20 to start doing some implementation work

1:46:21 with that coalition. Really excited

1:46:23 about that work. We obviously uh this

1:46:25 year expanded human services. We hired a

1:46:27 resource navigator. We launched CARES

1:46:30 Brookline which is the uh human services

1:46:32 resource database and referral system.

1:46:35 We have our Brookline Bounty program

1:46:37 that has for the past several years

1:46:39 through ARPA uh and other grants served

1:46:41 approximately 800 families each year

1:46:43 with food coupons for the farmers

1:46:45 market. And in addition, when we've had

1:46:47 the funding and through some fundraising

1:46:48 from the friends of Brooklyn Public

1:46:50 Health, we've provided turkeys and

1:46:51 chickens and and um other other food

1:46:55 during Thanksgiving and winter holidays

1:46:56 to families in need. And then it wasn't

1:46:58 in the chip, but I think it's important

1:47:00 to mention uh among many other things

1:47:02 that our staff do, we do provide uh flu

1:47:05 clinics each year to residents and and

1:47:07 this past year it was about 700

1:47:09 residents that received flu vaccine from

1:47:11 our staff.

1:47:13 So, really quickly, Emily Williams said

1:47:15 pictures are good, so I'm including a

1:47:16 couple pictures here. I I I promise you

1:47:18 all I will not quit my day job and get

1:47:20 into graphic design. I think uh

1:47:22 Christina, if she's watching at home, is

1:47:23 probably sobbing. Uh but I just wanted

1:47:25 to share a little bit about uh the the

1:47:28 wonderful staff we have. Right. This

1:47:29 work does not happen without the uh the

1:47:31 staff we have in public health and human

1:47:32 services.

1:47:36 This is more of our staff. Brookline day

1:47:38 vaccine clinics. Uh the diaper drive

1:47:40 that community health did the the

1:47:42 Brooklyn bounty program and on.

1:47:45 So what are our goals for FY27 and

1:47:48 beyond? Uh obviously

1:47:51 for this year it'll be the action the

1:47:53 year 2 action plan with CHIP. We're I'll

1:47:55 admit we're a little bit behind

1:47:56 schedule. I'm going to blame the

1:47:57 transition and leadership there on that.

1:47:59 Um but we are uh preparing to launch the

1:48:02 priority area working groups. So there

1:48:04 are four priority areas. We will have

1:48:06 residents and staff in each of those uh

1:48:09 priority areas in working groups helping

1:48:10 to develop that year 2 action plan and

1:48:12 making sure that we're we're staying on

1:48:14 track. I expect we'll see more or an

1:48:17 expansion of the work of the human

1:48:18 services resource navigator. You'll

1:48:20 remember we have the office of housing

1:48:21 stability. Um this position is supposed

1:48:24 to be doing that work. Um this past year

1:48:27 in town meeting there was the renters's

1:48:28 handbook that was passed. This position

1:48:30 will be taking on the responsibility of

1:48:32 starting to to develop that material.

1:48:36 And I want to continue to improve the

1:48:38 operations in our environmental health

1:48:39 team. We have a wonderful staff doing

1:48:41 inspections. they they're responsible

1:48:42 for both housing and food. Uh there's a

1:48:45 tremendous workload there, but I think

1:48:46 there's opportunities for for improving

1:48:48 operations so that we can respond better

1:48:50 to the residents of Brooklyn. In the

1:48:52 midterm, uh looking more at implementing

1:48:55 a robust public health regulatory

1:48:57 framework. I think one of my past

1:48:59 predecessors was um more comfortable

1:49:01 with passing public health regulations.

1:49:03 It's not something I want to do

1:49:04 frequently, but I think what we've seen

1:49:06 from town meeting over the past, you

1:49:07 know, 5 10 years is uh a demand for

1:49:11 public health to do more. And I think we

1:49:13 can do that through the uh the authority

1:49:15 of the the commissioner of public

1:49:16 health. Um so looking to do some more of

1:49:19 that around um banning new substances.

1:49:22 There's substances like synthetic

1:49:24 cannabonoids keep coming up. They come

1:49:25 up with new configurations of

1:49:27 cannabonoids. uh we can regulate those

1:49:29 through the public health department and

1:49:31 and stop uh allowing those to be sold in

1:49:34 our community. Uh keeping of animals uh

1:49:36 I think town meeting a couple years ago

1:49:38 I had a warn article on keeping of bees.

1:49:40 So that's the type of work I'd like to

1:49:42 do through the regulatory and and uh

1:49:44 regulatory work that I'd like to do and

1:49:46 I want to continue to review our our

1:49:48 operations and make improvements in in

1:49:50 key areas. I think there's a lot of

1:49:52 great work happening. I think we can

1:49:53 always do better. uh and I want to work

1:49:55 we we just hired a assistant director

1:49:57 for operations and uh that's Tanya Hong

1:50:01 her responsibility is really to go

1:50:02 division by division and department by

1:50:05 department that we work with and talk

1:50:06 about how do we improve communications

1:50:08 how do we develop SOPs as staff leave a

1:50:12 lot of information leaves with them and

1:50:13 we want to prevent that from happening

1:50:15 in the future and then long-term uh just

1:50:18 wanted to flag as I mentioned earlier

1:50:20 that um through a combination of of town

1:50:22 funds where they're available but also

1:50:24 So we'll continue to be applying for

1:50:25 grants wanting to continue to sustain

1:50:27 our current FDA FTEEs where possible. So

1:50:30 we have staff in community health, human

1:50:32 services, substance use prevention, and

1:50:34 our regional collaboratives that are all

1:50:36 on offsets and grant money.

1:50:38 And then lastly, uh if budget

1:50:41 limitations did not exist, right, um my

1:50:43 uh our emergency preparedness

1:50:45 coordinator is currently on soft money.

1:50:47 Uh she's doing tremendous work for this

1:50:49 town. She helps run the flu clinics with

1:50:51 our medical reserve corps. uh she does

1:50:53 some work during the marathon. Um she

1:50:57 just always out there in the community

1:50:58 educating seniors on on what they can do

1:51:00 to prepare for emergencies. So would

1:51:02 love to keep that position and move that

1:51:04 to the operating budget when and if

1:51:06 possible. And I know these are difficult

1:51:07 times. Uh but this is just flagging it

1:51:10 for the future. Um improving our grant

1:51:11 writing coordination. We're doing a lot

1:51:13 of great work on grant writing to

1:51:14 continue to bring in uh grants from

1:51:16 outside. I think we could do a better

1:51:18 job as a town on on coordination and

1:51:20 support there. want to continue to

1:51:22 expand social supports where we can. I

1:51:24 think our council on aging is working on

1:51:25 transportation that was in the community

1:51:27 health improvement plan. Continue to

1:51:29 expand where we can food and essential

1:51:31 support services and developing new

1:51:34 programs for families and teens. And and

1:51:36 as I've said a couple times now, just

1:51:38 always being mindful and preparing for

1:51:40 that future transition of staff from

1:51:42 expiring grants and offsets to new

1:51:44 grants and and operating budget where it

1:51:46 makes sense.

1:51:48 And then I had to mention next week's

1:51:49 National Public Health Week. We got a

1:51:50 lot of great events and with that I will

1:51:52 uh take any questions you have.

1:51:54 Great. Thank you. Any questions John?

1:51:57 Um thanks very much and um you know if

1:52:01 if people haven't sort of figured this

1:52:02 out from your presentation we have a

1:52:04 really robust public health department

1:52:06 and it's it's really great to know and

1:52:09 tremendous depth of professionals in the

1:52:11 department. Um I am going to raise this

1:52:14 issue of uh I'm I'm going to guess based

1:52:17 on your presentation that the health

1:52:19 department might in fact be um uh when

1:52:22 you rank all the departments by the

1:52:25 amount of their budget that is soft

1:52:27 money so-called u you know grants etc.

1:52:30 Um maybe the health department will come

1:52:32 out number one. Would you would you

1:52:34 guess maybe that's true?

1:52:36 I would say we're up there. I haven't

1:52:37 looked at a lot of the other budgets but

1:52:38 it's it's over a million dollars in soft

1:52:40 money.

1:52:40 Yeah. A a and um- which leads to my

1:52:44 question because um for very good

1:52:47 reasons. You know, you have described,

1:52:49 you know, one of the goals as being to

1:52:52 be able to continue by sometimes

1:52:55 incorporating into the operating budget

1:52:57 positions that have been on soft money.

1:53:00 But, you know, we we're going through

1:53:02 this exercise right now where we're

1:53:05 facing a sizable override uh that the

1:53:08 voters will vote on uh very soon. And um

1:53:12 the general message that comes through

1:53:15 the work of the expenses and revenues

1:53:16 committee and u when you look at all of

1:53:19 the causes of our our needing this

1:53:21 override funding um the message comes

1:53:23 through that we don't really have much

1:53:26 room for additions of personnel to our

1:53:30 budgets. Just having the same number of

1:53:32 people on our budgets from one year to

1:53:34 the next is is something we're having a

1:53:36 hard time sustaining without going to

1:53:38 overrides. So, is it realistic to be

1:53:41 talking about and I I know these are

1:53:44 really important public health um

1:53:47 functions, but is it realistic to be

1:53:50 talking about maybe growth of the

1:53:52 department through additions to the

1:53:53 operating budget from transitions, you

1:53:56 know, from soft money to operating

1:53:58 budget money? We do we really have room

1:54:00 to do that?

1:54:01 I think that's a difficult conversation

1:54:03 that needs to be continued to be had,

1:54:05 right? I I don't expect that this is the

1:54:07 year that that's going to happen. Uh

1:54:08 there's right now only

1:54:11 194,000 in grant funding that we're

1:54:13 losing in in FY27. Um and there's only

1:54:16 one staff person that was on that grant

1:54:18 and we have additional funding to keep

1:54:19 that person for now.

1:54:21 Uh this is more of a longer term having

1:54:23 that conversation. I mean if you look

1:54:24 back at FY23, 20 staff were on operating

1:54:27 and now it's it's uh

1:54:29 down to 12, right? So we've lost eight

1:54:31 FTEEs off the operating budget. So I get

1:54:34 that this is challenging. I just want I

1:54:36 felt like it was my responsibility to

1:54:37 flag this for everyone that it's great.

1:54:40 I you know I wish one of the biggest

1:54:42 projects we have uh from grant funding

1:54:44 is the regional collaborative and that's

1:54:46 with Arlington Belmont and Newton in

1:54:48 Brooklyn. And so we have five staff on

1:54:50 there plus a a part-time that we

1:54:52 actually pay out through our consultant

1:54:54 line because they work for Belmont. So

1:54:56 we have a public health nurse, an

1:54:57 epidemiologist, two inspectors, a

1:54:59 regional manager, and I'm probably

1:55:01 forgetting someone. So, so they're

1:55:03 they're providing a wealth of service to

1:55:04 all four communities right now. It would

1:55:07 have been ideal for Mass DPH when they

1:55:09 offered that grant to say to all four

1:55:10 communities, you need to be prepared in

1:55:13 five years that this funding may or may

1:55:15 not be here and you you've got to decide

1:55:16 whether you want to continue.

1:55:17 Yeah.

1:55:18 So, what I can say is for FY27, that

1:55:21 grant is fully funded. For FY28, that

1:55:23 will be the first year that the federal

1:55:24 government is not contributing half of

1:55:26 those dollars. So it remains to be seen

1:55:29 what that regional collaborative and the

1:55:31 regional collaboratives across the

1:55:32 state. I think close to threequarters of

1:55:35 of cities and towns are in regional

1:55:37 collaboratives how that's what that's

1:55:39 going to look like. Um

1:55:40 and if I may just one quick followup

1:55:42 because you mentioned that uh 20 U FTEEs

1:55:46 a few years back 12 today. Is that

1:55:48 mainly like that there was the COVID

1:55:51 period when a lot of money flowed in and

1:55:53 positions were added? Is that related to

1:55:55 that or?

1:55:56 Uh, no. In fact, um, we, you know, I

1:55:59 think the town was pretty smart when

1:56:00 when the arbor dollars came in. I think

1:56:02 only one position was added. Um, the

1:56:04 rest were,

1:56:06 you know, the transition has been using

1:56:08 opioid settlement funds. We were using

1:56:09 HCA funds. I don't think that maybe is

1:56:11 perfectly documented here, but we were

1:56:12 using HCA funds for certain substance

1:56:14 use prevention positions. We've now

1:56:16 transitioned them over to opioid

1:56:18 settlement funds. They had previously

1:56:20 been on a federal grant. So, this is

1:56:21 sort of this is public health and I get

1:56:22 it, right? We're always trying to be

1:56:24 competitive and get grants. The,

1:56:26 you know, and you're all very well aware

1:56:28 of this, the support from the federal

1:56:30 government right now is not what it was

1:56:32 a few years ago, especially as it

1:56:34 relates to public health. So, that's one

1:56:35 of the challenges is it's going to get

1:56:36 harder for us to find grants to continue

1:56:39 to support these positions.

1:56:40 So, you know, I think collectively we'll

1:56:42 have to face that. I just figured it was

1:56:45 time to start having that conversation

1:56:46 with you all in in town meeting. Thanks,

1:56:50 Michael.

1:56:50 Um, thank you very much. uh for your

1:56:53 presentation.

1:56:55 Uh just three quick questions. Um

1:56:58 actually just two because John already

1:56:59 asked one. Um

1:57:02 how much uh you talked about your grant

1:57:04 writing work and uh uh how you were

1:57:08 looking to enhance it um in I think your

1:57:12 midterm. And I'm wondering how much of

1:57:14 that grant writing work uh requires

1:57:17 specific knowledge within the public

1:57:19 health field um as opposed to grant

1:57:23 writing work that could be handled more

1:57:25 generally.

1:57:26 Yeah, that's a great question. I think

1:57:28 um we've got some staff members, you

1:57:30 know, I think it varies year to year as

1:57:32 staff transition in and out. Some of

1:57:33 them are very skilled at it and come in

1:57:36 prepared and others u not as much and

1:57:38 need more support. Um, I'd probably say

1:57:41 it's 75% specific public health

1:57:43 knowledge and 25% is kind of just

1:57:46 understanding where to look for grants,

1:57:48 how to how to apply for them, the the

1:57:50 type of language you need to use. We are

1:57:52 going to be doing a training for uh a

1:57:55 number of staff in the regional

1:57:56 collaborative including Brookline staff

1:57:59 on grant writing uh as a way to try to

1:58:01 support that internally. Uh, and we do

1:58:04 sometimes use contracted services. Um,

1:58:06 for some of the bigger grants, there are

1:58:08 some successful uh, consultants out

1:58:10 there who who do these for a living and

1:58:12 so sometimes we do hire them to support

1:58:13 some of that work, but I'd say it's

1:58:15 about 7525.

1:58:16 Okay. And my second question isn't

1:58:18 specific to grant writing, but your

1:58:20 operations more generally um, though it

1:58:23 might apply to grant writing is, uh, how

1:58:25 have you thought about the use of

1:58:26 artificial intelligence to improve the

1:58:28 efficiency of your operations?

1:58:30 Uh, I've thought a lot about it. I I I

1:58:33 think um it's early days, but I think

1:58:35 it's it's something we need to be

1:58:37 considering. And and I will just say

1:58:38 that I'm a a pilot tester of some of the

1:58:40 AI software that the town is is trying

1:58:42 out right now. Um so it's too early to

1:58:45 say, but I I think there's definitely a

1:58:46 lot of opportunity for AI. You know, I

1:58:48 think a lot of the questions that come

1:58:50 in from residents are things that

1:58:51 probably exist on the website, but the

1:58:54 search engine doesn't do a sufficient

1:58:55 job of pulling up the exact response.

1:58:57 And so I think and I'm not suggesting

1:58:59 that AI would be answering our phones

1:59:01 and and I don't think residents would be

1:59:02 happy to talk to a computer. I think

1:59:03 they'd rather talk to our staff. But it

1:59:05 may be that our staff can find the

1:59:07 answers quicker with support of AI. Um

1:59:10 we've got a lot of documentation I think

1:59:11 about, you know, our community health

1:59:12 improvement plan is a long document and

1:59:14 AI can comb through that really quickly

1:59:16 and find answers. And just quickly, um,

1:59:20 I'm curious, at one point, and this

1:59:23 predates your tenure, there was a chip

1:59:25 steering committee, and I'm just

1:59:27 wondering if if that's still engaged in

1:59:29 the planning and work of the chip.

1:59:30 Yeah, that that it still exists. The

1:59:32 same members are still part of it. We

1:59:34 have not met since June of last year.

1:59:36 That will be part of this spring's work

1:59:38 uh as we get ready for year two action

1:59:40 planning is to bring the steering

1:59:41 committee back. And then we still need

1:59:43 to appoint working group members. So the

1:59:44 steering committee is kind of leadership

1:59:46 team. They meet quarterly. the working

1:59:48 group is supposed to be meeting on a

1:59:49 monthly basis. So the what I can say is

1:59:51 staff are meeting on Friday to begin

1:59:53 that planning work. Um the year one was

1:59:56 handled by an outside consultant so

1:59:58 we're taking this on uh and so this is a

2:00:00 little new for us but we I feel like

2:00:02 staff are prepared.

2:00:03 Great. Thank you.

2:00:03 Yeah.

2:00:05 Uh David have a follow-up question on

2:00:07 the AI. Okay. So, you mentioned you

2:00:08 wouldn't want AI to be replacing uh

2:00:11 people answering the phone, but that you

2:00:13 would look to utilize it in order to

2:00:17 locate certain items on the website. Uh

2:00:19 do you do you envision any other uses

2:00:22 for AI within your department? I want to

2:00:25 be careful not to speculate too much,

2:00:26 but I you know I think um I was at a a

2:00:29 training uh the DPW commissioner had had

2:00:32 shared with us uh a little over a year

2:00:34 ago and it was it was somebody who's on

2:00:37 a on a commission and you know one of

2:00:38 the things they talked about is its

2:00:40 ability to look at images really quickly

2:00:43 and identify items in there. So I you

2:00:45 know this is pie in the sky. Don't, you

2:00:47 know, don't quote me or or force us to

2:00:49 do this, but like

2:00:52 sidewalks when there's snowstorms. You

2:00:54 could put cameras on all the vehicles

2:00:56 that are driving around town. They could

2:00:57 identify sidewalks that haven't been

2:00:59 shoveled, and you wouldn't need staff to

2:01:00 go out and then those staff could focus

2:01:02 on inspections where they're supposed to

2:01:03 be instead of sidewalk enforcement. So,

2:01:06 I think that's the type of potential,

2:01:08 but there's going to be an investment.

2:01:10 And I think there's also some concerns

2:01:12 probably from residents about cameras

2:01:14 picking up their images while those

2:01:16 vehicles are driving around town. So I

2:01:17 think that's an opportunity. I think,

2:01:19 you know, getting to knowledge quicker.

2:01:21 You know, I spend a lot of time reading

2:01:22 that chip and I'm very familiar with it,

2:01:24 but I still, you know, when I look at

2:01:25 like select board goals and objectives

2:01:26 on a yearly basis and I'm trying to line

2:01:28 it up with the chip, it takes a while.

2:01:30 Uh, and so I think their AI can look at

2:01:32 both documents and say here's where you

2:01:33 need to look and here's where they align

2:01:35 and here's where they don't. Um, so I

2:01:37 think I do think there's a lot of

2:01:38 opportunity. I think it's it's early

2:01:40 days and it we need to be really

2:01:42 thoughtful about the implementation.

2:01:43 So so the broader idea would be to make

2:01:46 employee time more efficient.

2:01:48 Yes. Yeah. And then I think you know I

2:01:51 think about you know and I want to be

2:01:53 mindful of of open meeting law and if

2:01:55 I'm going down the wrong road you guys

2:01:57 correct me. We had somebody at open mic

2:01:58 talking about rodents tonight. Um, you

2:02:00 know, if if our inspectors if we can

2:02:02 take a task off of our inspector's desk

2:02:04 or make it maybe faster for them to get

2:02:06 through an inspection, then we can

2:02:08 respond to complaints faster. I mean,

2:02:10 they they are swamped between food

2:02:12 inspections, housing inspections. We

2:02:14 have to respond to housing inspections

2:02:15 by statute in a certain amount of time.

2:02:17 So, then we're putting off food

2:02:18 inspections. We always have to respond

2:02:20 to housing inspections. I think I don't

2:02:22 I'm not going to give a date, but it's

2:02:23 it's very quickly you have to get back

2:02:25 on the housing inspections. and certain

2:02:26 ones are emergencies that need to be

2:02:28 responded to within 12 to 24 hours. And

2:02:31 so where you can take certain tasks off

2:02:33 their desk and make them, you know, make

2:02:34 it more efficient, then they can focus

2:02:36 more on some of their primary

2:02:37 responsibilities.

2:02:39 Thank you.

2:02:39 Yeah.

2:02:41 Good. Thank you.

2:02:43 Thank you.

2:02:43 I think you're going to stay up there

2:02:44 for the next item.

2:02:48 have one slide.

2:03:45 Hi everyone. Good evening. How are you?

2:03:48 Great.

2:03:48 We're good. How about you?

2:04:00 Um, let me just share my screen. I have

2:04:05 a presentation.

2:04:07 Um,

2:04:12 Melanie, I I'll be sharing my screen, I

2:04:14 believe. Right.

2:04:17 Yep.

2:04:18 Yeah,

2:04:18 we can see it.

2:04:18 Looks looks good.

2:04:20 Okay.

2:04:23 Uh, hi everyone. My name is Emily

2:04:25 Williams. I am just one year in to uh my

2:04:29 tenure at the Brooklyn Council on Aging

2:04:31 and Senior Center. And every

2:04:32 presentation I give, I have to start out

2:04:34 with the mission because it's as the

2:04:36 first council on aging. Sorry, Jim.

2:04:38 We're the first council on aging. 1955

2:04:40 we were founded. Um it's a very

2:04:43 important way to be able to share the

2:04:45 history to the public. Um we're uh

2:04:48 twofold. We have a senior center which

2:04:51 is one of the uh beautiful town

2:04:52 buildings and it's the gathering place

2:04:54 specifically dedicated to enriching the

2:04:56 experience for Brooklyn older adults age

2:04:59 6 plus as well as their caregivers. I

2:05:01 will say we're seeing definitely an

2:05:03 uptick in the uh adult children giving

2:05:05 us a call, worried about mom or dad um

2:05:07 and their well-being and emotional um

2:05:10 overall health. Our mission uh at the

2:05:13 Brooklyn Senior Center is to provide

2:05:14 social services that that allow people

2:05:17 to remain independent. Our goal is

2:05:19 helping people maintain this

2:05:21 independence so that they continue to be

2:05:23 active members of their community. And

2:05:26 we believe that enhancing growth and a

2:05:28 sense of belonging is inc while

2:05:30 encouraging individual contribution and

2:05:33 the sharing of life experiences is vital

2:05:35 to remaining as a part of uh the

2:05:37 community. The senior center welcomes

2:05:41 all. We're very um culturally diverse

2:05:44 group not only uh with our participants

2:05:47 volunteers but also the staff. Everyone

2:05:50 who enters our doors, regardless of age,

2:05:52 income, disability, ethnicity, or

2:05:54 religious background, is very welcome.

2:05:56 And lastly, we recognize the efforts of

2:05:58 all of our program collab collaborate

2:06:01 collaborators,

2:06:02 and we are extremely grateful for their

2:06:04 support.

2:06:08 um our FY27 budget summary which was um

2:06:12 not a dramatic change. This is not a a a

2:06:15 change in um finances for us on the town

2:06:18 budget. It is for the federal budget. Um

2:06:21 but we do have our our salaries. You can

2:06:23 see year-over-year. Uh most recently um

2:06:27 just a small less than 1% increase in

2:06:30 our salaries. services, supplies remain

2:06:33 stable and our utilities did increase by

2:06:37 5%. Which reflects overall 1% increase

2:06:40 in our total budget

2:06:44 and that 1% increase is from our new

2:06:46 HVAC replacement. We're all electric

2:06:49 very soon will be 100% complete with the

2:06:52 the process. Um they replaced the entire

2:06:56 system and the provider that's been

2:06:58 doing the work has been very respectful

2:07:00 of the participants. I will say and not

2:07:02 disrupting any service to our

2:07:04 operations.

2:07:07 Uh this year are our three priorities.

2:07:10 You can see on the left we have a brand

2:07:11 new town logo. We also have uh which

2:07:14 we're in the process of redesating the

2:07:16 town of Brooklyn to be an age friendly

2:07:18 community. But overall our priorities

2:07:21 this year will be health and safety,

2:07:24 equity and aging

2:07:26 in the community. And then it's really I

2:07:28 should have separated the the the third

2:07:31 uh priority into two to to list it as

2:07:34 sustainable infrastructure and

2:07:36 innovation. Uh we've done quite a few uh

2:07:40 different things this year to reflect

2:07:41 innovation. That includes creating a new

2:07:44 database for folks to check in. It's

2:07:46 called my senior center. Um now you'll

2:07:48 see when you walk in the front doors a

2:07:50 little kiosk um where you can enter in

2:07:53 your information if you like. It's opt

2:07:55 in. You don't have to and you can pick

2:07:57 and choose where you're going, what

2:07:59 program you'll be in attendance to, and

2:08:01 then we'll be able to really put

2:08:03 concrete data to the usage of the senior

2:08:06 center building as well as our programs

2:08:08 and services.

2:08:17 um this year. Um it it's kind of funny

2:08:21 because we've seen an expansion in our

2:08:22 programs and um utilization of our

2:08:25 services

2:08:27 uh with a pretty stable budget. So it's

2:08:30 reflective in our infrastructure. You

2:08:33 can see here we have 31 uh staff which

2:08:36 served just over 3,000 unduplicated

2:08:39 older adult residents. There's over

2:08:40 13,000 as we know, 20% uh who live in

2:08:43 town age 60 plus. One thing that I'm

2:08:47 very proud of, more proud than you can

2:08:49 even imagine, um there's 42,000 hours,

2:08:52 which is equivalent to 20 full-time

2:08:54 employees.

2:08:55 That is 370 volunteers, which equates to

2:08:59 $1.3 million, which is the our town

2:09:03 general fund budget. And my first year,

2:09:05 what I've done is I've tried to

2:09:07 determine really what the true core

2:09:09 service need is

2:09:10 and try to reflect that into the true

2:09:13 core service budget. and we cannot do

2:09:16 what we do at all without our

2:09:18 volunteers. Um, next month we're

2:09:20 celebrating uh volunteer appreciation

2:09:23 month. Uh John, thank you for coming to

2:09:25 say a few words, but everyone is very

2:09:27 welcome to be able to see who these

2:09:29 people are, all ages, um that are, you

2:09:32 know, meeting the needs of people who

2:09:35 walk in and are looking for services and

2:09:37 supports or who just want to uh a very

2:09:40 affordable cup of coffee, but also to

2:09:43 really have that social connection

2:09:45 because we're the hub of all things

2:09:46 older adult in town. Um our senior

2:09:49 center is 20,000 square feet. It's

2:09:52 really

2:09:53 not appropriate to meet the needs right

2:09:55 now of a growing population. Every day

2:09:58 we're busting at the seams. On average,

2:09:59 we have about 15 programs to be able to

2:10:02 address food, nutrition, socialization,

2:10:05 mental, physical, and um uh in-person

2:10:08 wellness, but also what we're doing is

2:10:11 we're trying to explore more hybrid

2:10:13 programming. But one thing we're

2:10:14 noticing is that the the older adults

2:10:16 really prefer to be in person.

2:10:19 Um, other ways we've expanded. We've

2:10:21 sustained growth

2:10:24 with a huge thanks, huge shout out to

2:10:26 our Brooklyn Senior Center Foundation.

2:10:28 They fund um just 10% of our overall

2:10:32 budget. Um the the town funds 70% 20%

2:10:37 are through external sources, special

2:10:39 revenue funds, state earmarks, grants

2:10:42 that we apply to internally. Um as well

2:10:45 as uh of course we all know about the

2:10:47 ARPA cliff. Um, so those numbers will be

2:10:50 changing in the next fiscal year, but

2:10:52 the Brookline Senior Center Foundation

2:10:54 funds every single program that we offer

2:10:57 at the senior center as well as staff

2:10:59 positions. For example, one of our band

2:11:01 drivers is funded through them as well

2:11:03 as our fitness center coordinator. Um,

2:11:07 and we're hiring for that position right

2:11:08 now. Quick side note, uh, we've had 20%

2:11:11 of our staff either retire or resign due

2:11:14 to different reasons. um one individual

2:11:17 left the country unfortunately because

2:11:19 of the current federal situations that

2:11:21 we're faced with. So I've never actually

2:11:23 seen such a dramatic change in uh

2:11:26 leadership in an organ organization. But

2:11:28 knock on wood, we've filled almost all

2:11:31 those positions as well as hired um

2:11:33 internally. We promoted someone to be

2:11:35 our our new supervisor of uh clinical

2:11:38 social work services

2:11:40 to continue on programs and

2:11:42 communications. We're very proud. Um I

2:11:45 think Brookline in general is very proud

2:11:46 to to to be an aging community. Uh we've

2:11:49 seen some fantastic folks really step up

2:11:52 to the plate to advocate for the future

2:11:54 existing as well as the future needs of

2:11:57 aging in Brooklyn. Um, and that's not

2:11:59 just being a community advocate. It's

2:12:01 attending our programs to be able to

2:12:03 take care of themselves as well as as

2:12:05 well as their communities in our solo

2:12:08 and wise aging classes, our fitness

2:12:10 classes, and of course, lifelong

2:12:12 learning. Um, one thing that I'm really

2:12:15 proud of, um, John, thank you for

2:12:16 throwing in all those pictures. We have

2:12:18 a new electric vehicle that we'll be

2:12:20 piloting with the senior center, excuse

2:12:22 me, the teen center to be able to

2:12:24 provide intergenerational and interdep

2:12:26 departmental uh transportation again to

2:12:29 meet the needs of the growing community.

2:12:32 And then we're always expanding townwide

2:12:35 initiatives interdep departmentally as

2:12:37 well as with uh local nonprofits in

2:12:39 town. Our top three accomplishments,

2:12:43 we've strengthened health and safety net

2:12:44 programming. Um again, we've met an

2:12:47 increased demand in our health caregiver

2:12:49 and social service supports and we're

2:12:51 trying to advance and really trying to

2:12:54 modernize the future needs. Um

2:12:57 population growth brings technology and

2:13:00 we're slowly but surely uh embracing

2:13:02 that just to try to embrace this baby

2:13:05 boomer ch population who uses their

2:13:08 their smartphones for many different

2:13:10 purposes. Um, and it all of this is to

2:13:13 help folks remain safe and independent.

2:13:15 Um, the second uh accomplishment we've

2:13:18 done is to expand the equity and

2:13:20 community connections in town where

2:13:22 we've done really inclusive programming.

2:13:25 Um, in November, I think everyone

2:13:27 remembers the snap cuts and where

2:13:29 everybody had to remobilize and pivot on

2:13:32 how to respond um effectively and

2:13:35 creatively to meet the needs. And now we

2:13:38 have a a monthly uh group with uh other

2:13:40 town departments

2:13:42 and other nonprofits in town to be able

2:13:45 to meet that need for essential services

2:13:47 like food. Um we've also done as I

2:13:50 mentioned a lot of hybrid engagement

2:13:51 opportunities to reduce the social

2:13:53 isolation that we see on a daily basis.

2:13:56 And lastly, our top three

2:13:58 accomplishments advancing transportation

2:14:00 and an age friendly progress. So we've

2:14:03 sustained key access to limited

2:14:05 transportation services. We've had to um

2:14:08 put together some uh new restraints to

2:14:11 our current transportation model for uh

2:14:14 the medical to meet the medical needs of

2:14:16 older adults where we have uh limited

2:14:18 ride trips on a monthly basis and now

2:14:20 our model is an incomerestricted model

2:14:24 and the goal of that is to be able to

2:14:25 sustain the existing funding that we

2:14:27 have to fund that program. We've also

2:14:30 done great work on initiating the age

2:14:32 friendly redesignation with AAP which is

2:14:35 a process that's done every three years.

2:14:37 Um and as the age friendly cities

2:14:40 committee is a subcommittee of the

2:14:42 select board. We look forward to what

2:14:43 that looks like.

2:16:57 very significant

2:16:59 uh aging population. I include myself in
