2:17:30 Thanks. I appreciate that.

2:17:33 Uh yes, thank you Emily for a very

2:17:35 thorough uh and uh helpful report. Um

2:17:39 I'm wondering if you could just speak

2:17:41 for one moment to any of the

2:17:43 intergenerational work um that the

2:17:46 senior center uh is championing. Um, I'm

2:17:50 familiar with the with the uh teen

2:17:52 center, senior center shuttle, but I'm

2:17:55 just wondering if there's uh if you have

2:17:58 any thoughts about uh expansion of

2:18:01 intergenerational connections.

2:18:04 Well, having just been for the first

2:18:06 time to the Brooklyn Arts Center last

2:18:08 week, what a what a gem that is. We uh

2:18:11 recently received a state earmark uh

2:18:13 from Tommy Pollo to do some

2:18:15 intergenerational arts. Um, creative

2:18:17 arts therapy reduces isolation and

2:18:20 loneliness and really addresses the

2:18:21 mental health needs that we have in our

2:18:23 community. Sometimes they're not talked

2:18:25 about enough in my opinion. But that's

2:18:27 one example of a way to bridge two

2:18:29 communities, the old and the young, and

2:18:32 to be able to almost have that ven

2:18:34 diagram to see where their needs and

2:18:36 their um hobbies and their um

2:18:39 relativities to one another um can can m

2:18:42 be matched. And then again putting data

2:18:45 to that for sustainability and future

2:18:48 programming. It's just magical when you

2:18:50 get an older adult and a younger person

2:18:51 together. Um we have the um shop grocery

2:18:55 shopping program, the shop program which

2:18:57 started uh in the late 80s with the high

2:19:00 school. Um, and on our website we have,

2:19:03 and I just watched it today, is a really

2:19:05 heartwarming video of some high school

2:19:07 students grocery shopping for a woman

2:19:09 who couldn't grocery shop any longer and

2:19:11 how it's it's not so much about the

2:19:14 food, but it's about the friendship that

2:19:16 develops. Um, those are just two

2:19:18 examples of ways to get um to bridge the

2:19:21 community really across the lifespan.

2:19:24 And there could be more.

2:19:25 Thank you, David. Regarding expansion,

2:19:29 I'm wondering if there is any thought

2:19:32 toward uh having wheelchair accessible

2:19:36 vehicles to take seniors to and from

2:19:39 appointments. Uh I know there's some

2:19:41 interest in that.

2:19:45 Absolutely. Absolutely. Our current um

2:19:48 contracted service with uh a local taxi

2:19:51 company does not have wheelchair

2:19:52 accessible vehicles as part of their

2:19:54 fleet, but we hope to use for medical

2:19:57 transportation

2:19:58 um enhanced outreach and education to

2:20:01 the ride. It's not the ride that we're

2:20:03 used to even just 10 years ago. Um but

2:20:07 what they've um been able to expand

2:20:09 their services to provide microtransit.

2:20:11 It's called the Flex program and it's

2:20:13 really a great way to for seniors who

2:20:15 use their smartphones or to call us to

2:20:18 be able to access um a a subsidized town

2:20:21 subsidized service for folks in

2:20:24 wheelchairs and if they have their

2:20:26 personal care attendant or loved one to

2:20:29 go with them. It's always a nice um

2:20:32 opportunity to get people to and from

2:20:34 their doctor's appointment. But all of

2:20:36 our vans um which are aging I will point

2:20:39 out are wheelchair accessible including

2:20:41 our new electric vehicle.

2:20:45 So one thing that um I I find

2:20:48 fascinating is the number of social

2:20:49 workers you have on staff five um plus

2:20:53 uh part-time social worker funded with

2:20:56 grants. Could you uh just give people a

2:20:59 sense of you know what what what

2:21:02 your social work um group uh does for

2:21:08 elderly people particular

2:21:11 workers but also the uh other uh

2:21:14 individuals who provide supportive help

2:21:17 to uh to people like home care workers

2:21:19 and and others.

2:21:22 Absolutely. We currently employ five

2:21:24 clinical social workers and we just

2:21:26 filled our last position with our our

2:21:29 intern from Boston College. She's

2:21:31 amazing. She'll be joining us full-time

2:21:33 soon as she graduates. Um it's been a

2:21:35 trend actually. I will say folks um in

2:21:38 the social work profession start with us

2:21:40 as interns, enjoy it so much because of

2:21:43 the plethora of ex of uh exposure to

2:21:46 services and a variety of older adults

2:21:49 needs that they can address. Um, one

2:21:52 thing I am very uh conscious of is to

2:21:54 make sure that folks don't burn out as

2:21:56 the crisis interventions increase. Um,

2:21:59 we're relaunching the inter agency

2:22:01 meetings um with our public health

2:22:04 department as well as police, fire, and

2:22:07 uh the Brooklyn um mental health uh

2:22:10 Brooklyn community for mental health.

2:22:12 There's really we need to address social

2:22:15 isolation in general, but what we're

2:22:18 seeing is um the depression increase as

2:22:21 well as pretty severe mental health um

2:22:24 concerns, hoarding

2:22:27 um infestations and public housing as

2:22:30 well as in private housing. Um, and what

2:22:33 we're doing with our our clinical team

2:22:34 is to address those needs really at the

2:22:38 at the social work intervention level,

2:22:40 which we don't go in to someone's home

2:22:43 in a uniform. We don't go looking to

2:22:45 section someone. We go to support the

2:22:48 individual based on their needs and

2:22:49 really meet them where they're at. And

2:22:51 that's one fantastic trait of a social

2:22:54 work uh uh employee. We also have a

2:22:57 great um uh volunteer team. We have the

2:23:01 tax workoff program in Brooklyn which we

2:23:03 employ uh over 30 residents age 60 plus

2:23:07 who are homeowners and what that does is

2:23:10 able to match them into a town

2:23:11 department to be able to supplement um

2:23:14 additional employee needs and that's

2:23:17 been incredibly successful not just at

2:23:19 the council on aging but the library uh

2:23:22 town hall the assessor's office and it

2:23:24 really helps the older adult be able to

2:23:26 work off their taxes. Um, we also are

2:23:29 addressing caregiver stress with our our

2:23:32 staff. So, we have a plethora of

2:23:34 resources in our toolkit. to be able to,

2:23:37 you know, have at the ready, not just

2:23:39 online, but when we have our walk-ins at

2:23:41 the senior center, to be able to address

2:23:43 those needs immediately, to have the

2:23:45 answer for somebody is is is vital to

2:23:47 their well-being because our goal is to

2:23:50 empower everyone to be able to go back

2:23:52 to their homes, to go back to their

2:23:54 family to say, "This is what I recommend

2:23:57 kind of thing." And that our case loads

2:23:59 of each social worker as as well as

2:24:03 existing staff can range between 20 to

2:24:05 40 clients per person. Um which is is

2:24:09 really um increasing and high. I was

2:24:12 looking at the history of the senior

2:24:14 center and you know we were able to grow

2:24:16 this uh division within the council on

2:24:18 aging from a halftime position up to

2:24:21 five position but it you know it took

2:24:24 over 40 years to be able to get there

2:24:26 and it's uh still with a 20% population

2:24:30 in town. Um we also have wonderful

2:24:34 transportation coordinators. We have two

2:24:37 part-time um assistants as well as a

2:24:40 full-time transportation coordinator and

2:24:44 um almost all of those positions are

2:24:46 funded by special revenues um as well as

2:24:49 the Brooklyn uh senior center

2:24:51 foundation. So, one of my goals in the

2:24:53 long term is to be able to bring the

2:24:55 coordinator to the town budget 100%

2:24:58 because of that transportation need. Um

2:25:01 and then there's Jamie who does

2:25:03 incredible programming. Her and her team

2:25:06 offer programming not just at the senior

2:25:08 center as I mentioned 15 programs daily.

2:25:11 We also have a daily lunch program. We

2:25:13 also have um bi-weekly trips and uh what

2:25:16 we've done in the most recent is to go

2:25:19 down to the Butterham Library to meet

2:25:21 the the needs of the South Brookine

2:25:23 residents who we hear on a daily that

2:25:26 they are um just not coming up to

2:25:28 Koolage Corner because they can't find

2:25:30 parking for more than two hours

2:25:32 or our van they just choose not to to

2:25:36 use our van or they're not accessible

2:25:38 via bike or Uber and Lift. So um what

2:25:42 I'll be doing this fiscal year is to

2:25:44 looking at the data that we are taking

2:25:47 in to be able to address the true need

2:25:50 um based on that data for expanding

2:25:52 services. We can't go backwards. We have

2:25:54 to go forwards.

2:26:00 Okay. Thank you Emily.

2:26:03 Very very good.

2:26:05 Thanks.

2:26:08 Okay. Next, uh, we have the library and

2:26:11 our new director, Jill Mccurio.

2:26:14 Hi. Good evening,

2:26:59 All right. Good evening. It's so nice to

2:27:01 be here in person to meet all of you. Uh

2:27:05 I started about two months ago and I

2:27:08 have to say I've learned so much about

2:27:11 how Brookline relies on their library.

2:27:14 I'm excited to walk you through our

2:27:15 budget presentation and also to share

2:27:17 the impact that we have on community

2:27:20 life. So let me just start my slideshow.

2:27:24 And these were in your packet as well.

2:27:29 So, here we go. I like to say the best

2:27:32 way to support a public library is to

2:27:34 use it. So, that is something that uh

2:27:37 Brooklyn residents do enthusiastically.

2:27:40 Uh last year we welcomed about a half a

2:27:43 million visitors to the public library.

2:27:45 That's up 4% just about from the prior

2:27:48 year. and our circulation combining our

2:27:51 physical and digital borrowing. Uh we're

2:27:54 seeing that come in around 1.1 million

2:27:57 items borrowed annually and our digital

2:28:00 borrowing just continues to grow. We

2:28:04 hosted 1,200 programs roughly last year

2:28:07 attended by 30,000 residents. And the

2:28:10 most important thing to keep in mind is

2:28:12 the library is open seven days a week um

2:28:16 and year round. and we have about 175

2:28:21 hours public service hours available

2:28:23 throughout all of our locations. This

2:28:26 level activity would not be possible

2:28:29 without our dedicated team. So they uh

2:28:33 the team of about 55.66 FTEEs to be

2:28:36 precise, they've expanded partnerships,

2:28:39 they champion access to information, and

2:28:41 they've promoted community connection

2:28:44 inclusion throughout all of their

2:28:45 activities this year.

2:28:48 So the first action item that we worked

2:28:50 on this year was an art walk through

2:28:53 college corner. So we took the library

2:28:55 outside of its library walls and brought

2:28:58 art and community together at the

2:29:00 Brooklyn Art Festival. The staff

2:29:02 organized a self-guided uh art walk

2:29:05 through Coolage creating a passport uh

2:29:07 that guided residents through galleries

2:29:09 and studios along the route. Uh it was

2:29:12 such a success that this morning the

2:29:14 staff met to figure out how they could

2:29:15 participate this year again in the

2:29:17 Brooklyn Art Festival. We also surveyed

2:29:20 residents and heard from about 200 folks

2:29:22 who were interested in coming to

2:29:24 afterhour programming. Uh we hosted four

2:29:27 different events trying to appeal to all

2:29:30 ages. So we had movie screenings, some

2:29:33 trivia, there was a Taylor Swift music

2:29:35 party and I think our most successful

2:29:38 one is the pictures on the screen. We

2:29:40 had 50 uh residents come out uh to

2:29:43 decorate boxes. The kiddos um made them

2:29:46 look like drive-in cars and they parked

2:29:48 it in Henman Hall and watched family

2:29:50 movies together. Um we also promoted our

2:29:54 digital resources. So the digital

2:29:57 resources are a huge component of our

2:30:00 collection budget. Um, and our digital

2:30:02 circulation went up 30% uh or I'm sorry,

2:30:05 it went up 8% this year through some of

2:30:07 the tactics that we did in this action

2:30:09 item. We know that um people really want

2:30:14 to take advantage of our services for

2:30:16 ebooks and e audiobooks through Canopy

2:30:19 and um through Libby, but I think that

2:30:23 there's a lot of other digital resources

2:30:25 that will be exciting to residents and

2:30:27 they just need to know about them. So,

2:30:29 this action item took um our staff and

2:30:32 made tutorials, did uh training at the

2:30:34 housing authority and the senior center

2:30:36 to try to help people understand how to

2:30:38 get better use out of these tools. So,

2:30:40 we have services like Knos that gives

2:30:43 you free access to music. Uh there's

2:30:46 learning platforms like Mango Languages

2:30:48 that you can learn a new language.

2:30:50 Creative Bug has uh tutorials on

2:30:53 different craft projects, but I think

2:30:55 one of the most special ones that we

2:30:57 have is um Press Reader, which is a tool

2:31:00 that kind of helps you get behind the

2:31:01 payw wall. So, you can read free

2:31:03 articles from the New York Times, Boston

2:31:05 Globe, USA Today, and and popular

2:31:08 magazines and newspapers from around the

2:31:11 globe. And we saw that Brooklyn borrowed

2:31:15 43% more articles in the last year by

2:31:18 using this service. The last um part on

2:31:22 this slide is our library of things

2:31:24 collection and that is a really special

2:31:26 unique collection that draws upon the

2:31:30 idea that to access knowledge it's not

2:31:32 going to fit in one book or one

2:31:34 database. uh you need to sometimes put

2:31:36 your hands on an object so you can teach

2:31:38 yourself a new skill or learn to fix

2:31:41 something in your home or maybe to bond

2:31:43 with a friend or family member. So this

2:31:45 collection we did some work to analyze

2:31:48 the usage, try to figure out how to best

2:31:50 take care of the collection and uh I'm

2:31:52 happy to say in 2025 the library things

2:31:56 collection circulated 20,000 times. So,

2:31:59 these popular items include the

2:32:01 Chromebooks, games, gadgets like in

2:32:04 induction cooktops, and we have a pretty

2:32:06 impressive library of tools at the

2:32:08 Koolage CL um corner location. The

2:32:12 library is wrapping up its five-year

2:32:14 strategic plan, which is guided by four

2:32:17 pillars that reflect our commitment to

2:32:19 education, equity, partnerships, and

2:32:22 access. So, we support lifelong

2:32:24 learning. We partner with organizations

2:32:26 to help expand the reach of the library.

2:32:29 And we believe the library is for

2:32:30 everyone. So, it's our responsibility to

2:32:33 build a collection, have programs and

2:32:35 services that reflect the diversity of

2:32:37 our community, their interests and

2:32:39 information needs. And we also want to

2:32:41 help residents continue to use the

2:32:43 library whether they're on the go or

2:32:44 from the comfort of their own home. So,

2:32:47 what's on the horizon for the library in

2:32:49 the next year? Well, we do need to

2:32:51 rebuild the team. Um, I have a learning

2:32:53 curve to get to know Brookline and to

2:32:55 find out what we're doing well and ways

2:32:57 we can expand our services, but we also

2:33:00 have uh had a lot of turnover in our

2:33:02 team. I'm happy to say that we're now at

2:33:06 a more stable part in our staffing. Uh,

2:33:08 in the last year, we hired eight

2:33:11 full-time positions and 17 part-time

2:33:13 positions.

2:33:14 Uh, but threequarters of that uh

2:33:17 full-time positions were internal

2:33:19 promotions. So retention is strong and

2:33:22 we're working together to learn how to

2:33:25 work as a a healthy team together.

2:33:27 There's a new strategic plan on the

2:33:29 horizon. I hope that you'll all

2:33:31 participate in it later this fall so we

2:33:33 can find out how we can shape the best

2:33:35 library going forward. And then sharing

2:33:38 everything that we do so all residents

2:33:40 know what they can access. I'm looking

2:33:42 forward to having a monthly newsletter

2:33:44 and finding other tools to help the

2:33:46 community know what they can access and

2:33:48 take advantage of for free through the

2:33:50 public library. Um, and then let's see,

2:33:53 we've got

2:33:55 a couple facts about library budgets

2:33:57 just before we go into our numbers. Uh,

2:34:00 the library budgets a little unique. We

2:34:02 are guided by certain uh statutory

2:34:05 regulations in Massachusetts where we

2:34:08 have to meet different funding

2:34:09 requirements and provide a set number of

2:34:12 services so we are eligible for

2:34:15 certification that allows Brooklyn

2:34:17 residents to borrow materials throughout

2:34:19 the minute network but also the

2:34:20 Commonwealth and to make sure that we're

2:34:22 eligible for state aid that further

2:34:24 expands our ability to improve library

2:34:27 services outside of the municipal

2:34:29 budget. Uh so we need to uh follow a

2:34:32 funding threshold uh formula. That's the

2:34:35 municipal appropriation requirement. We

2:34:37 need to make sure our collections are

2:34:39 funded at a certain level of our budget.

2:34:41 Uh and then we have to have a set number

2:34:44 of services. We have to be open to the

2:34:46 public for a variety of hours um and

2:34:48 have a a variety of program

2:34:50 opportunities. Um so that's important

2:34:52 for us to maintain certification for the

2:34:55 community. So, as we dive into our

2:34:57 library budget, our budget proposal this

2:34:59 year is for $4,778,400.

2:35:04 Um, we're seeing utility costs rise just

2:35:07 like all of my other departments that

2:35:09 you've heard from. Uh, our our ser our

2:35:12 capital expenses are going up for leased

2:35:15 equipment and supplies are going up. Um,

2:35:18 so our budget, while it increases by 1%

2:35:22 this year, it is offset by a decrease to

2:35:24 our part-time staffing law.

2:35:26 Um, and that's something that we're

2:35:29 going to be looking at our vacancies

2:35:31 really carefully because we want to

2:35:33 maintain our staff. We want to be able

2:35:35 to maintain our core services. Uh, in a

2:35:39 perfect world, we'd be able to have more

2:35:41 staff because they're directly tied to

2:35:43 all the programs and services that we're

2:35:44 able to offer the community. Um, but we

2:35:48 we do know that this is a difficult

2:35:50 financial time. Uh, so we'll be

2:35:52 strategic about looking at that and we

2:35:53 want to make sure that the Brooklyn

2:35:55 community still has um the library that

2:35:58 they expect and deserve. Uh, so through

2:36:01 our talented staff and through our

2:36:04 community partnerships, the public

2:36:06 library brings the community together.

2:36:08 They champion access to information and

2:36:12 this budget will allow us to continue to

2:36:14 provide the wonderful library services

2:36:16 that the community has been relying on.

2:36:19 Uh so thank you for your attention uh

2:36:22 and supporting the library. I welcome

2:36:24 any questions you have about our

2:36:26 services or our budget proposal.

2:36:29 Great. Thank you.

2:36:31 Oh.

2:36:32 All right. Thank you. Um and welcome.

2:36:35 Thank you so much.

2:36:36 We're glad to have you. I'm glad to be

2:36:38 here.

2:36:39 My family frequents the library. They

2:36:41 love it, my kids. Um,

2:36:43 so you mentioned that there was a 4% uh

2:36:46 increase in visitors last year verse, I

2:36:49 guess, year-over-year. Yeah.

2:36:50 Um, how are we doing on um hours? Are we

2:36:54 are hours the same? Are they decreasing?

2:36:56 Are they increasing?

2:36:58 Our hours have maintained the same. So

2:37:00 that 175 hours is across all the

2:37:02 locations. We're open 63 hours to the

2:37:06 public

2:37:08 uh each week. So sometimes we overlap

2:37:11 often at different locations. That's

2:37:12 more in people's neighborhood, easier to

2:37:14 get to. Um but our hours really haven't

2:37:16 changed. Um we try to make sure we have

2:37:18 a mix of evenings available, weekend

2:37:21 hours on Saturday and Sunday, so all

2:37:25 everyone in the community can help take

2:37:26 advantage of what we have going on. So,

2:37:28 while you've increased 4% yearonear,

2:37:31 your budget's gone up 1%, you're still

2:37:33 maintaining the same level of service.

2:37:34 Uh, we're very good at being thrifty in

2:37:37 the public library. We're we believe in

2:37:39 sharing. Uh, so it is a challenge with

2:37:42 so many people coming through our doors

2:37:44 all the time. Um, and you know, frankly,

2:37:47 for people looking for things to do

2:37:48 after school or in school vacation week,

2:37:51 those programs get in, you know,

2:37:52 particularly slammed. Uh, but we are

2:37:55 still working with what we have. We're

2:37:57 very fortunate to have our foundation.

2:37:59 We have a runner for team Brooklyn, just

2:38:01 to mention uh and our friends group and

2:38:04 our trusts that uh our trustees manage.

2:38:06 Those help us fill in those gaps.

2:38:08 Great. Well, thank you. And we're

2:38:09 fortunate to have you.

2:38:10 Oh, thank you so much,

2:38:13 David.

2:38:14 I know that uh several years ago you

2:38:16 started a a tools library at the Koolage

2:38:19 Corner Library. I'm wondering what other

2:38:21 types of innovative concepts have maybe

2:38:24 come up for what a library can be for

2:38:27 its community.

2:38:28 Oh, that's a huge question. Uh the

2:38:31 library things continues to be all the

2:38:33 rage. I will say across libraries and I

2:38:36 would say that the articles that we get

2:38:38 asked to do the most with local papers

2:38:40 tend to be trying to highlight this

2:38:42 collection. I believe um we just had a a

2:38:46 university come and wanted to ask what's

2:38:48 up with the tool library in Kulage

2:38:50 because they're trying to promote it to

2:38:51 their student base um who study

2:38:54 frequently at the college corner

2:38:55 library. Um in terms of things to put in

2:38:58 there, I mean we have VR headsets. I

2:39:00 mean there's lots of games. Um and when

2:39:04 you say game you you might just think

2:39:06 pure recreation, but sometimes they're

2:39:08 tools that help build conversations with

2:39:10 people. Uh, our ELLL program has kits so

2:39:14 people could check out uh a dictionary,

2:39:16 but also a cookbook and and things that

2:39:19 will help them do a real life task, but

2:39:22 lean on some resources from the library.

2:39:24 Um, there there's all sorts of things in

2:39:26 that collection that can just make

2:39:28 everything um so much richer for people

2:39:31 at home without having to invest a lot

2:39:33 of money. Uh, our databases are also

2:39:35 really interesting. that press reader

2:39:37 tool. If you haven't used it, I totally

2:39:39 recommend you pop on our website, try it

2:39:41 out because if you've ever been

2:39:43 frustrated about not getting access to

2:39:45 something, most of those major uh

2:39:47 journals are in there. Um, but I think

2:39:49 right now it it shouldn't be

2:39:51 revolutionary, but access to information

2:39:53 is getting harder and finding good

2:39:55 information is even harder. So, your

2:39:57 question about AI um rang with me a lot

2:40:01 because, uh, we want to try to help

2:40:03 people use the tools available as best

2:40:04 we can. So, I think that's an area that

2:40:06 the library can do more in to try to

2:40:08 help u make sense of the information

2:40:12 confusion that is out there right now.

2:40:14 Thank you,

2:40:16 Joey.

2:40:18 Welcome to uh Brooklyn.

2:40:19 Thank you. Um, and I'm going to say a

2:40:21 word on behalf of the databases that are

2:40:25 available through the library because I

2:40:27 think that uh a lot of people don't are

2:40:29 not aware of of just how rich uh a part

2:40:33 of the library collections that is. And

2:40:36 um two that I reference frequently

2:40:41 um are the Brooklyn annual reports

2:40:43 databases. You can search every single

2:40:46 Town of Brooklyn annual report from 1846

2:40:48 to 2017. Um the others, in case people

2:40:52 are wondering, are available through the

2:40:53 town website. And then um personal

2:40:56 favorite of mine, the Brooklyn newspaper

2:40:58 archive. Um I spent 10 years as the

2:41:01 editor of a paper called the Brooklyn

2:41:03 Chronicle Citizen. we and um don't want

2:41:06 to date myself too much, but in those

2:41:07 days the the way we archived the

2:41:10 newspaper was to save a copy every week

2:41:13 and then by the end of the year you'd

2:41:15 have a great big bound volume and I mean

2:41:17 it was huge. Um, and then we'd set aside

2:41:21 a couple more copies just sort of in a

2:41:23 pile. And when I um left after 10 years,

2:41:27 I thought I don't I can't bear the

2:41:29 thought that all of the work that went

2:41:31 into the newspaper for the 10 years I

2:41:33 was editor will be lost to me. and and I

2:41:36 hauled all these newspapers and kept

2:41:39 them in our basement for more years than

2:41:42 I wish to recite until all of a sudden

2:41:45 one day I woke up and realized I don't

2:41:47 need to do that and I can search every

2:41:50 article that was published by every

2:41:52 reporter um and every article I wrote

2:41:54 from my time at that newspaper, you

2:41:57 know, in no time at all. And if people

2:42:00 don't fully appreciate newspapers are

2:42:03 great, you know, uh, sources for for

2:42:06 local history and and you can find some

2:42:09 really fascinating local history just by

2:42:11 going through the newspaper archive. And

2:42:13 there's more than one newspaper, not

2:42:15 just the one I edited, but then there

2:42:17 were su successor newspapers. So, thank

2:42:20 you. I'm not really asking you for a

2:42:21 comment, but I'm just telling telling

2:42:23 people of Brooklyn, this is another

2:42:25 great resource at the library. I'm so

2:42:27 glad we were there to preserve that with

2:42:29 you. So, thank you.

2:42:31 Yeah. One thing that I'm really

2:42:33 fascinated by is the 3D printer,

2:42:36 although I've never gotten my act

2:42:38 together to go over and and learn um how

2:42:41 to use it and what it's used for. Can

2:42:43 you talk about that?

2:42:45 Yeah, a lot of people love the 3D

2:42:47 printer. So, if you come to the Brooklyn

2:42:49 Village location on the ground level by

2:42:51 the elevator, you could see it in action

2:42:54 printing off jobs that people have

2:42:56 requested. Uh, and people are very

2:42:58 creative. There's also a display case

2:42:59 right next to it. So, you can see some

2:43:01 of the projects that have uh people have

2:43:04 created. So, everything from, you know,

2:43:07 a a little screw that will fit in uh

2:43:10 your glasses to being able to keep

2:43:12 things going uh to cookie cutters or u

2:43:15 artistic work. It's really pretty

2:43:17 impressive. So, what you do is you're

2:43:20 learning about how to design uh a

2:43:22 three-dimensional object that will be

2:43:24 printed on a platform and the design

2:43:27 programs, many of them are free that you

2:43:29 can use and tinker with. And then you

2:43:31 see the the product of your work to see

2:43:34 if you got those dimensions correct. Um

2:43:37 I also am a novice at 3D printing, but

2:43:40 we have some staff that are absolutely

2:43:42 fantastic at it. So, if it's something

2:43:43 you want to learn, uh, we can help you,

2:43:46 uh, learn, see a tutorial to get you off

2:43:49 the ground. We do programming around it,

2:43:51 uh, but it will also be able to help you

2:43:52 print it for free in the end, too, which

2:43:55 is, uh, unusual. That's not always

2:43:57 offered in a lot of public libraries.

2:43:59 So, please come in. We'll help you out.

2:44:00 And please see the display case. When

2:44:02 you see it in action, you're like, "Wow,

2:44:04 I could be that creative, too."

2:44:10 Okay. Thank you.

2:44:11 Thank you so much. interesting and

2:44:13 welcome to Brooklyn.

2:44:14 I appreciate it. Thank you so much and

2:44:16 have a great evening.

2:44:17 You too.

2:44:20 Uh, next, Department of Public Works. I

2:44:23 think um, Commissioner Shoots on

2:44:26 the whole team is online.

2:44:28 Okay.

2:44:32 Good evening.

2:44:36 So, uh, we'll go ahead and pull up our

2:44:39 slides. Um,

2:44:42 I know you're probably taking that down.

2:44:46 Um,

2:44:48 but I guess, okay, I'll just get

2:44:50 started. So, uh, good evening, chair,

2:44:52 members of the select board. I'm pleased

2:44:53 to be here with, uh, the incredible DPW

2:44:56 leadership team to present the

2:44:58 Department of Public Works FY27 budget.

2:45:01 Public works touches nearly every part

2:45:03 of daily life in Brooklyn from streets,

2:45:06 sidewalks, parks, water and sewer,

2:45:08 sanitation, trees, sustainability, and

2:45:11 emergency response. For FY27, our focus

2:45:14 is straightforward. Maintain core

2:45:17 service delivery, improve the condition

2:45:19 of our assets, strengthen internal

2:45:21 capacity, and continue modernizing how

2:45:24 we serve the public. The DPW operating

2:45:27 budget is 21.1 million, an increase of

2:45:31 about 1.6% over FY26.

2:45:35 The water and sewer enterprise budget is

2:45:38 35.6 million, an increase of about 3.1%.

2:45:42 Across both budgets, we've worked to

2:45:44 preserve service delivery, absorb fixed

2:45:46 cost pressures where possible, and

2:45:49 continue making targeted investments in

2:45:51 core operations and infrastructure. The

2:45:54 DPW budget reflects a cut of two FTEEs

2:45:57 and some services. The increase in

2:46:00 contractual obligations includes

2:46:02 personnel steps and um other contractual

2:46:05 services uh largely grounds maintenance

2:46:07 and sanitation. Uh this includes not

2:46:10 only trash and recycling tonnage but

2:46:12 also non-CAL increases and hazardous

2:46:15 waste, white goods, mattresses, compost,

2:46:18 yard waste, etc. Uh we did receive a

2:46:20 question at advisory around how much was

2:46:22 associated specifically with trash um

2:46:26 collection and that's $69,000

2:46:29 of that in FY26. I would highlight three

2:46:32 major accomplishments. Uh first, we

2:46:35 successfully transitioned to the town's

2:46:37 new trash collection uh model, which is

2:46:40 providing strong service to residents

2:46:42 while also allowing town staff to focus

2:46:45 more capacity on other important public

2:46:47 works services. Second, we continued to

2:46:50 strengthen our asset management

2:46:51 approach, including roadway and sidewalk

2:46:53 planning, maintenance, prioritization,

2:46:55 and long range capital coordination. And

2:46:58 third, we invested in our work through

2:47:00 force through internal promotions,

2:47:02 in-house CDL training, technical and

2:47:04 safety training, and stronger interdep

2:47:06 departmental and regional coordination,

2:47:08 including water and sewer emergency

2:47:10 response training with MWR.

2:47:13 For FY27,

2:47:15 our work is organized around six cross

2:47:18 department priorities. uh communication

2:47:20 and customer service, staffing,

2:47:23 retention and capacity building, asset

2:47:25 management and maintenance,

2:47:26 environmental health and safety, digital

2:47:28 transformation, and climate resiliency

2:47:30 and environmental sustainability. These

2:47:32 priorities help us manage a broad and

2:47:35 complex department in a more coordinated

2:47:37 and accountable way for detailed goals

2:47:40 and accomplishments across each

2:47:41 division. We have about 140 in the

2:47:43 budget book that support these

2:47:45 priorities.

2:47:47 Within these six priorities, our FY27

2:47:50 focus is on practical improvements that

2:47:52 support both service and accountability.

2:47:54 On customer service, that includes

2:47:56 improved communication tools, community

2:47:58 engagement, and continued strong

2:48:01 performance in trash collection. And

2:48:03 working on improved and predictable

2:48:05 community engagement protocol on

2:48:07 staffing, it means growing internal

2:48:08 capacity through training and

2:48:10 promotions. And with asset management,

2:48:12 it means keeping roads, sidewalks,

2:48:14 parks, and utility systems in a state of

2:48:17 good repair using data and life cycle

2:48:19 planning.

2:48:21 For safety, it means stronger

2:48:23 onboarding, investigations, and updated

2:48:26 policies. For digital transformation,

2:48:28 we're looking at moving more permitting

2:48:30 and record systems to modern platforms.

2:48:33 And regarding climate and

2:48:34 sustainability, it means moving from

2:48:36 planning to implementation through the

2:48:38 climate action and resiliency plan, the

2:48:40 open space and recreation plan, and

2:48:43 vision zero plan and others. So for

2:48:46 FY27, our priorities are closely aligned

2:48:49 with the select boards. We're supporting

2:48:52 implementation of the town's major

2:48:53 plans, including the climate action

2:48:56 resiliency plan uh that you will see in

2:48:58 the coming weeks. uh as we think about

2:49:00 cost efficiencies and value uh overall I

2:49:04 want to underscore underscore that our

2:49:05 sustainability and natural resources

2:49:07 function has brought in 3.2 million to

2:49:10 the town since FY24

2:49:13 and just received notice that our

2:49:14 application met the rigorous climate

2:49:16 leader community requirements opening

2:49:18 access to over 1 million in energy

2:49:21 efficiency and renewable energy funding

2:49:23 per year to the town. This division is

2:49:25 also leading the solar expansion

2:49:28 initiative that will result in

2:49:29 significant reduced costs for the town

2:49:31 over time.

2:49:33 Another significant place of alignment

2:49:35 with the select board's road map and

2:49:37 priorities is what we're continuing um

2:49:41 is that we're continuing to improve

2:49:43 Brook Lion's roadway and sidewalks

2:49:45 through a costbenefit valuebased asset

2:49:48 management approach. Um this is one of

2:49:51 the primary uh goals for the select

2:49:53 board. Um, we've completed our analysis,

2:49:57 our conditions assessment of the roads

2:49:58 and determined the most fis fiscally

2:50:01 proactive and responsible approach for

2:50:03 maintaining the roads with the funding

2:50:06 available. Uh, this year Hammond and

2:50:08 Harvard will be rehabilitated. The

2:50:11 overall um cost uh for the roads is

2:50:15 about $100 million uh for the renovation

2:50:19 of roads. Uh the roads are mapped by

2:50:22 treatment and by year uh for the best

2:50:25 value for the town. Uh and then uh we're

2:50:29 also looking at this slide specifically

2:50:32 just to show you kind of a change over

2:50:34 time. Um it shows the change in roadway

2:50:37 maintenance work since 2016 through

2:50:41 2025.

2:50:42 And this is specifically looking at

2:50:45 square yards of paving.

2:50:48 Um and the highest year or last last

2:50:52 year 25 uh we had about four miles. So

2:50:56 that that was about four miles worth of

2:50:58 paving uh whereas in FY16 it was less

2:51:02 than half a mile. So the pavement the

2:51:05 select board's pavement maintenance and

2:51:07 management policy has enabled

2:51:08 maintenance work to go forward based on

2:51:11 this model while also asking the

2:51:13 transportation board and staff to

2:51:16 prioritize critical complete street

2:51:18 vision zero safe routes to school and

2:51:20 bicycle accommodation projects. Um so as

2:51:24 you can see as we look forward we'll

2:51:26 continue on that uh strong trend.

2:51:29 Sidewalk and ramp work is also strong.

2:51:32 You heard from Jim Lee, chair of the

2:51:33 Commission on Disabilities earlier about

2:51:35 improvements in this area. We're working

2:51:38 ahead of the roadway work and

2:51:39 incorporating not only the conditions

2:51:41 assessment, but also priorities from the

2:51:43 Commission on Disability and the

2:51:44 Pedestrian Advisory Committee. We're

2:51:46 incorporating those into the annual work

2:51:48 plan. We are thrilled to see a

2:51:51 significant increase in our staff's

2:51:54 ability to do this work inhouse as well.

2:51:56 in FY25.

2:51:58 Uh we were only able to accomplish about

2:52:00 a quarter of a mile um worth of sidewalk

2:52:03 work. Uh and this year um we'll be over

2:52:06 two miles of sidewalk work completed

2:52:08 with this crew and then we're looking to

2:52:10 grow that by another third uh in the

2:52:13 year ahead. So we're really glad that

2:52:15 we'll be able we're able to focus uh on

2:52:18 some of the um in-house talent that we

2:52:21 have uh to provide some of this work for

2:52:24 the town. And we are stretching town

2:52:26 dollars with our trip hazard removal

2:52:29 program. We're working with a contractor

2:52:31 who inspects the sidewalks, documents

2:52:33 trip hazards that fall within the range

2:52:35 that they can address, usually a/ inch

2:52:37 to 2 in, documents what the town needs

2:52:40 to replace, and then slices the sidewalk

2:52:43 with a precision blade that is far more

2:52:45 effective than um grinding. We completed

2:52:49 13 miles of sidewalk uh in 25 and to

2:52:53 date in 26 and we're looking at 20 new

2:52:57 roads for the balance of 26 and through

2:52:59 27. This is estimated to save 80% uh

2:53:04 over full sidewalk replacement. We may

2:53:07 need to go back and replace the sidewalk

2:53:09 in time, but it's improving the

2:53:11 condition and extending its useful life.

2:53:13 And uh this next slide is just a before

2:53:17 and an after picture. So if you um if

2:53:20 you see this, you'll know that this has

2:53:22 been inventoried and is part of our

2:53:25 program now to remove these trip

2:53:27 hazards. Um we also have very ex

2:53:30 exciting complete street work happening

2:53:32 in the public way such as Washington

2:53:34 Street, the bridal path, Lincoln school,

2:53:36 safe routes to school, traffic calming.

2:53:38 These are all moving forward and some

2:53:40 leveraging significant state and federal

2:53:43 dollars.

2:53:46 And I would be remiss in not recognizing

2:53:49 the 170 men and women of public works

2:53:52 that support Brooklyn every day and also

2:53:55 their incredible work. Uh this winter it

2:53:58 was a challenging season. It was also

2:54:01 incredibly um cold in addition to uh

2:54:05 some tough uh snow events.

2:54:09 So um here's our season recap. They did

2:54:11 an excellent job this winter with very

2:54:15 little sleep and a lot of snow removal.

2:54:20 Um and I don't know if you've seen this

2:54:23 image of the um DPW yard. This is our

2:54:26 facility up at the transfer station. Um,

2:54:29 this was actually not at its peak. Um,

2:54:33 but the the transfer station in this uh

2:54:36 snow dump, it's it's really invaluable

2:54:38 during the winter season to help the

2:54:40 town res resume day-to-day operations um

2:54:44 following significant snow events. Snow

2:54:47 removal is essential for public safety,

2:54:49 teacher parking, commercial area

2:54:51 parking, opening up intersections,

2:54:53 sidewalk ramps, and parking lots. So,

2:54:55 it's really important and helpful that

2:54:58 this is something that we do um with our

2:55:02 own site and facility because hauling um

2:55:05 the snow out of town would be incredibly

2:55:08 expensive.

2:55:10 So, uh in closing, the FY27

2:55:14 budget is designed to do three things.

2:55:16 uh protect essential daily services,

2:55:18 improve condition and management of the

2:55:20 town's assets, and continue modernizing

2:55:23 the way the department operates and

2:55:26 serves the public. Um the division

2:55:28 directors are here and we're glad to

2:55:32 answer any questions that you may have.

2:55:35 Thank you.

2:55:37 Questions from the select board?

2:55:42 John,

2:55:43 I'll jump in.

2:55:45 um Aaron and and the the division heads,

2:55:48 thank you so much. And I I do hope

2:55:50 people realize that uh the selectboard

2:55:53 review of this budget is going to be

2:55:55 cursory compared to the advisory

2:55:56 committee review which I happened to

2:55:58 witness the other night and it went on

2:56:02 for more than two hours, right? I'm not

2:56:04 exactly

2:56:04 four. It was four and that was our

2:56:07 fourth budget meeting.

2:56:08 Yeah. I mean it did talk about going

2:56:12 through a budget, you know, with a comb,

2:56:14 fine tooth comb, cliche, sorry. Um the

2:56:18 the advisory committee uh did did a

2:56:20 terrific job of uh checking on every

2:56:23 single aspect of your operations. And I

2:56:26 I I really recommend to people that they

2:56:28 if they've got the time, check out that

2:56:34 on. you will understand more about the

2:56:57 take for granted. you know, the trash

2:56:59 pickup, but also just the the the

2:57:02 ability to enjoy the a walk on a

2:57:04 sidewalk, the the the condition of our

2:57:06 streets that we use every day. Um, it it

2:57:09 really is impressive and I I can't say

2:57:11 enough about um the the work that the

2:57:13 department does. At the same time, I

2:57:16 have a question. Sorry for the long

2:57:18 preface. Um I I am I I I'm very pleased

2:57:22 that the select board in recent years

2:57:25 through policy emphasis has you know

2:57:28 worked with the DPW to shift more and

2:57:31 more resources into uh a stepped up

2:57:35 program of addressing the needs of our

2:57:37 streets. And you showed that in a slide.

2:57:39 It went by pretty fast. People might not

2:57:41 have fully appreciated what that slide

2:57:42 showed, but it really did show dramatic

2:57:45 increases going forward in the number of

2:57:48 streets we're going to be able to

2:57:49 address. That that is where I love to

2:57:52 see the the emphasis of the DPW. Where I

2:57:55 have doubts is on a couple of these um

2:57:58 one one in particular um the the Beacon

2:58:08 at this point, you know, all we know

2:58:13 planning and coordination uh with the

2:58:22 might be done. I don't think we have a

2:58:24 clear indication of whether this is even

2:58:28 feasible, you know, gi given the layout

2:58:31 of uh the streets as as they exist today

2:58:34 and the use of the streets for parking

2:58:36 areas uh for trees uh you name it. um

2:58:42 are at what point will we get a sense of

2:58:45 how much this is going to cost us

2:58:47 altogether

2:58:49 and at the end of the day do we have a

2:58:52 real good handle on how much it will be

2:58:55 used because I've heard people say they

2:58:58 love the sidewalks

2:59:00 and they don't really feel any need for

2:59:03 a path that is adjacent to a trolley

2:59:06 track and then I've heard other people

2:59:08 say they would love to have exclusive

2:59:10 use of the uh the the Breen Beacon

2:59:14 Street bridal path for high-speed bike

2:59:17 commuting. Well, that doesn't mix.

2:59:19 That's not something that mixes in a

2:59:21 multimodal way, you know, with people

2:59:24 walking their kids, you know, or

2:59:26 strolling or el older people walking at

2:59:29 a slow pace. So, do we even agree on

2:59:32 what this is going to be used for? I I

2:59:34 I'm puzzled by it and all I know is

2:59:37 we've already spent, you know, we've

2:59:39 already appropriated a million dollars.

2:59:41 Have we spent any of that money? That's

2:59:43 the other thing that kind of puzzles me.

2:59:44 Uh when you consider how little there is

2:59:46 to show for this, are we spending that

2:59:48 money?

2:59:50 That's a lot.

2:59:51 I know. Um sorry, I went on a

2:59:54 and you know, and as you know, this has

2:59:56 been supported by town meeting and was a

2:59:59 grassroots effort. So, um there is a um

3:00:03 a broad uh design review committee that

3:00:06 has been established. I think that we're

3:00:09 going to have to figure all of that out

3:00:11 together. There is not a we we don't

3:00:21 that will evolve through the process.

3:00:25 um the design team. So tool um

3:00:30 is the consultant working on this uh has

3:00:33 been doing a lot of the sort of

3:00:35 groundwork on um sort of the the base

3:00:40 concept working on pulling together a

3:00:43 lot of safety related data over the last

3:00:45 10 years regarding um crashes,

3:00:48 incidents. And we have a roadway safety

3:00:50 audit um and meeting that's coming up

3:00:54 with mass DOT and whole police fire.

3:00:57 It's a huge group of people that really

3:01:00 look at safety associated with current

3:01:02 conditions and then ultimately that is

3:01:07 design. I think you put forward a lot of

3:01:15 with in terms of uh of the bridal path

3:01:20 itself, use of the bridal path, access

3:01:34 And we're going to have to do a lot of

3:01:36 thoughtful design about um how we would

3:01:44 town will have time to to decide um

3:01:48 whether it's something that they want to

3:01:50 uh move forward with. And I would say we

3:01:52 would have a better sense of cost

3:01:55 probably around 25% design. At this

3:01:58 point, the consultant team still has

3:02:02 additional survey work to do. Um, and

3:02:06 it's been quite complex in terms of

3:02:10 around the tas uh and other utility work

3:02:16 itself uh is taking an extended time

3:02:19 once they have that base information.

3:02:21 We've already done a lot of information

3:02:23 gathering from the community in terms of

3:02:25 defining the project, getting

3:02:26 information from the community in terms

3:02:28 of kind of their hopes and dreams about

3:02:30 it. But once we have the survey and some

3:02:32 base concepts, then um the public

3:02:35 process will really be off and running.

3:02:37 So the public will have something to

3:02:38 respond to, which I think is is

3:02:40 important.

3:07:50 is largely dealing with the interface of

3:08:01 that we assemble as a town. So, um, not

3:08:04 only speaking to the question of the use

3:08:40 to present to us. I I see a lot uh in

3:08:50 are now done fully with uh AI

3:08:54 um and

3:08:57 you know I think we'll certainly be

3:08:59 looking at services for us right now

3:09:01 it's a lot of the

3:09:03 actual technologies standard operating

3:09:07 procedures sort of writing standard

3:09:08 operating procedures um looking for

3:09:10 discrepancies between um sort of

3:09:14 multiple different procedures and being

3:09:17 able to do that very quickly and

3:09:18 optimizing uh those procedures I think

3:09:21 is a way that we'll use um AI and also

3:09:27 uh turn by turn um

3:09:31 directional optimization for example for

3:09:35 snow removal and having um you know

3:09:39 tablets in each vehicle which is giving

3:09:43 the plow drivers the exact routes. It's

3:09:46 giving us the documentation on where you

3:09:49 know what's been plowed, how many times

3:09:51 at what times, but also then when we

3:09:54 have to bring someone down for uh either

3:09:56 downtime or arrest or make adjustments,

3:09:58 we then make quick quick shifts and can

3:10:02 make sure that we are um optimizing uh

3:10:06 that work. And that'll apply also for

3:10:08 street sweeping and documenting

3:10:10 catchbasin cleaning and uh so I think

3:10:13 both the documentation and making sure

3:10:15 that uh we're we're being as thorough as

3:10:18 we need to be.

3:10:24 David. So, I have a question about the

3:10:27 uh how sanitation

3:10:29 is currently working out, whether you

3:10:32 anticipate any changes moving into the

3:10:35 next fiscal year uh given some of the

3:10:38 challenges at um the the center that we

3:10:42 have by

3:10:48 change over to Cassella.

3:10:50 Okay. So, I'll I'll start and then

3:10:53 Kevin, if you want to um jump in. So, as

3:10:56 this board knows, we um have a a sort of

3:11:01 a structural uh deficiency in the

3:11:04 transfer station floor. It's a building

3:11:06 that's 75 years old and um the floor has

3:11:11 been worn down um and to a point at

3:11:14 which uh we now need to construct a

3:11:17 temporary transfer station uh which

3:11:20 we're doing with a um temporary uh sort

3:11:23 of uh struct it's an armature type frame

3:11:28 building uh that'll enclose the

3:11:30 operation while we um work on a

3:11:33 full-time solution. So, I will say um

3:11:36 that our contractor has been incredibly

3:11:39 flexible with us. Um we had to sort of

3:11:42 stop their operation and have them haul

3:11:45 our trash out direct for several days

3:11:47 while we worked with D to um get

3:11:50 authorization to use one of our open

3:11:52 bays um to have the trash brought in

3:11:54 daily and then um resume the operation

3:11:58 in that location until we could

3:12:00 construct this um this facility. So once

3:12:03 we do this and it will be uh temporary

3:12:06 um

3:12:08 we don't see any changes uh in the short

3:12:12 term and uh nor do we see any changes at

3:12:16 this point until we're able to construct

3:12:18 that building which we hope will be done

3:12:21 uh this fall.

3:12:23 Do you anticipate needing any broader

3:12:26 changes at the transfer station? I know

3:12:28 there had been discussions prior to this

3:12:31 collapse of possibly needing a

3:12:32 significant renovation of that whole

3:12:35 facility.

3:12:36 Yes, we do believe that the transfer

3:12:39 station itself is long overdue for

3:12:42 replacement. Uh so that is something

3:12:45 that we'll be um analyzing through the

3:12:49 feasibility study. um that won't be a

3:12:52 full um design or cost estimate, but

3:12:55 just the overall um value to the town.

3:12:59 We have we actually have plans uh for a

3:13:03 new transfer station from when the

3:13:05 landfill was capped in the early 2000s.

3:13:08 So, it it was the intent to actually

3:13:10 replace the building then. um but cost

3:13:13 estimates exceeded um what had been set

3:13:16 aside for the capping and so um the

3:13:20 building wasn't uh reconstructed at that

3:13:23 time. So um

3:13:27 I think that

3:13:31 I think that's going to be a community

3:13:32 conversation, you know, around around

3:13:35 that new building and the value long

3:13:37 term to the town. We we think it's

3:13:42 a an asset and provides the town

3:13:45 flexibility that we wouldn't want you to

3:13:47 lose in the long term.

3:13:50 Thank you.

3:13:52 Cheers. And just quickly to follow up on

3:13:54 that, um, one of the things you

3:13:56 mentioned last year as an issue when we

3:13:58 were talking about moving to, uh,

3:14:00 increase Cassella's share of the, uh,

3:14:04 um, uh, sanitation services was that

3:14:07 there were folks who was whose time was

3:14:10 being taken up by other by sanitation

3:14:13 who were assigned to other activities.

3:14:15 Do you have a sense of, you know, how

3:14:17 that increase in sidewalk work for this

3:14:20 from last year to this year um or from

3:14:23 the prior year to the whatever it is two

3:14:25 years ago to this year um is related to

3:14:28 the fact that you now have a little more

3:14:30 flexibility there. Do you have a sense

3:14:32 of how much of that is is related to

3:14:33 that?

3:14:34 Yeah, Kevin.

3:14:37 Yes. Uh um we've seen some um tremendous

3:14:40 improvement on the other services that

3:14:43 we provide for the town that we weren't

3:14:45 able to do in the past due to the

3:14:47 staffing needs on sanitation. Uh for a

3:14:50 quick example, um the commissioner went

3:14:53 over uh quickly tonight, but in the past

3:14:56 two years, we might have uh poured

3:15:00 less than 100 uh cubic yards of concrete

3:15:03 uh with our in-house crews for the whole

3:15:06 year. And from July 1st to present,

3:15:09 obviously, till the winter, we're we're

3:15:12 a little over 300 yards. And our goal is

3:15:15 to maybe get up to 600 yards by July.

3:15:17 So, that's a huge improvement just on

3:15:20 the sidewalk maintenance. We were able

3:15:22 to put crew uh uh crew down in the

3:15:26 commercial areas repainting all the

3:15:28 light poles, the the street light poles,

3:15:30 and uh uh stuff that we've been trying

3:15:32 to do for years just to prevent the

3:15:35 maintenance on a lot of our assets that

3:15:36 we're able to um accomplish now. So,

3:15:40 yeah, we've seen a tremendous um

3:15:42 improvement on that. And and I'd be

3:15:46 remissed not to say that our morale at

3:15:49 the highway has improved a lot as well

3:15:52 because people

3:15:55 are coming in to do what they were hired

3:15:57 for every day.

3:16:03 Great.

3:16:03 Thanks. Good question.

3:16:04 Go ahead.

3:16:05 One more. Uh sorry. Um just to talk a

3:16:07 little bit about water and sewer and

3:16:09 sustainability

3:16:11 um storm water management and the um the

3:16:14 pipe work that we've been the the pipe

3:16:16 inventory that we've been doing not just

3:16:17 lead pipes but also the the storm water

3:16:20 system. I don't know if Jay and or

3:16:22 Alexandra want to talk about that in the

3:16:24 context of the the car

3:16:26 or if you I can I can jump in first. Um

3:16:30 thanks for the question. Yeah, we've

3:16:32 been moving right along with our, you

3:16:35 know, our utility asset programs. First

3:16:37 one, uh, the sewer. We've been

3:16:39 continuing our INI program where we go

3:16:42 in and we remove sources of clear water

3:16:44 out of the sewer system. And we do that

3:16:46 by aligning the pipe, basically putting

3:16:48 a pipe inside a pipe and sealing the

3:16:50 manhole. So, whenever we do that, it

3:16:53 basically cuts off the water's ability

3:16:55 to get into the sewer. And everything

3:16:57 that enters that pipe has to go to the

3:16:59 treatment plant. So any clear water that

3:17:01 you can keep out, you know, keeps our

3:17:03 cost down and it it saves, you know, the

3:17:06 treatment cost, treatment process, and

3:17:08 everything else. On the water side,

3:17:10 we've been continuing our sort of get

3:17:12 the lead out program. You know,

3:17:14 fortunately in Brooklyn, um when they

3:17:17 built out this town, it was mostly iron

3:17:19 pipe, so we really didn't have any

3:17:21 actual lead containing pipes. However,

3:17:23 we do have what they call lead

3:17:26 gooseeneck material, which is a flexible

3:17:28 piece of lead that connects the iron

3:17:30 pipe to the actual cast iron man.

3:17:33 And we're trying to just be proactive

3:17:35 and just get rid of those materials.

3:17:37 There approximately like 1,600, more

3:17:40 like probably 14,500 now. We've been

3:17:43 aggressively removing them and we're

3:17:45 trying to do that with um the planned

3:17:47 work that engineering and highway are

3:17:50 doing on on the roads that are coming

3:17:51 up. So the last thing we want is to have

3:17:53 an old service line pop after we've

3:17:56 paved the road and then we just look

3:17:58 foolish. So it's a great program,

3:18:01 cooperative effort and it's it's going

3:18:03 well. On the storm water front, um we've

3:18:06 been doing a lot of work there trying to

3:18:09 look at areas that are floodprone. We're

3:18:12 done with our storm water model and

3:18:14 we're working on our storm water master

3:18:15 plan to identify those floodprone areas

3:18:18 and come up with recommendations on how

3:18:20 to mitigate um you know potential

3:18:23 sources of flooding. We're also working

3:18:26 on an aggressive um elicit discharge and

3:18:29 detection program where we're trying to

3:18:31 locate and remove sources of bacteria.

3:18:33 Uh we appreciate um the previous

3:18:36 approvals for the funding there. We've

3:18:38 been actually able to contract with a

3:18:40 consultant and expedite a lot of that

3:18:43 work and make a lot of progress. So,

3:18:45 that's a real quick overview on water,

3:18:48 sewer, and drain, the three flavors

3:18:49 there. And, um, I guess I'll turn it

3:18:51 over to Alexandra if she's ready if she

3:18:53 wants to add anything on the

3:18:54 sustainability front.

3:18:58 Sure, thank you. Um, I will just say,

3:19:01 you know, Jay gave a great summary there

3:19:02 of all the work. Um but specifically

3:19:04 with the u storm water model and and

3:19:08 plan that the team has been working on,

3:19:10 we're really trying to integrate those

3:19:11 recommendations into our climate action

3:19:14 and resiliency plan. Um really helping

3:19:17 to highlight and prioritize for

3:19:19 resiliency, you know, some of our

3:19:21 greatest threats in Brookline as it

3:19:23 relates to climate hazards including

3:19:25 heat resilience um and uh storm water

3:19:28 and flood management as well as extreme

3:19:30 weather events. So, um, those are

3:19:32 incorporated into our resiliency

3:19:33 section. Um, and we're, uh, looking

3:19:36 forward to sharing that with the board

3:19:37 here in the in the next couple of weeks.

3:19:39 Thanks.

3:19:42 Great. Thank you.

3:19:44 John, did you ask one more?

3:19:46 Sure. Um

3:19:48 Chestn Hill Avenue um has gotten a lot

3:19:51 of attention uh in recent months and

3:19:56 recently which is that there is now

3:20:45 the reason given um which I totally

3:20:48 accept

3:20:50 for um not proceeding uh with with the

3:20:56 plan is that the the intent was to

3:21:15 Avenue, Brooklyn, and then Boston. Makes

3:21:23 to continue to be um applied to these

3:21:34 incorporate a um you know thoroughly

3:21:39 be guided by whether there's

3:21:40 connectivity to um lanes um at either

3:21:44 end um as it happens I guess they would

3:21:48 in most cases be in Boston.

3:21:52 Uh that's a great question. So I think a

3:21:55 few things about Chestnut Hill Avenue.

3:21:57 Chest Hill Avenue was on our list and uh

3:22:02 really kind of got ahead of itself

3:22:05 before the policy was finalized. And so,

3:22:09 um, while Chestnut Hill Avenue is due,

3:22:11 um, for just pavement maintenance, it's

3:22:14 probably, you know, as you know, it's

3:22:15 it's had a lot of public engagement. And

3:22:18 so, I believe that the transportation

3:22:21 board and staff still need to make a

3:22:25 decision on um what they want to do sort

3:22:28 of short-term, long-term at Chesh Hill

3:22:30 Avenue. And Dan, if that's not the case

3:22:32 and they've made a decision, let me

3:22:34 know. That was my latest that was my

3:22:36 understanding. Um we always try to

3:22:39 partner with um both Newton and Boston

3:22:43 where we can in terms of connectivity.

3:22:45 You know what projects they have that

3:22:47 they're working on, what we're working

3:22:49 on and how to make them uh work

3:22:51 together. So right now um you know is we

3:22:56 have our costbenefit value approach just

3:22:58 for maintenance of roads. Uh the

3:23:07 all of the other really important

3:23:11 accommodation, full complete street, uh

3:23:25 subcommittees and the public and staff

3:23:30 kind of a first cut of a rubric that

3:23:32 will help, but connectivity is going to

3:23:34 be part of that and then they'll

3:23:36 prioritize

3:23:37 um the work and the work has to be um

3:23:56 Hill Avenue is still um yet to be

3:24:25 27.

3:24:27 FY27.

3:24:29 Yeah.

3:24:29 Which would maybe start in 26.

3:24:33 FY27

3:24:35 starts in 26. Yes.

3:24:36 Yeah. Yeah. Okay.

3:24:44 Yeah. Okay. Thanks.

3:24:45 Yeah.

3:24:47 Okay. Thank you, Commissioner Shu.

3:24:50 Thank you. Nice to see you all. Thanks

3:24:52 to the incredible public works team.

3:24:55 Yeah. Yeah.

3:24:55 Thank you.

3:24:56 We agree.

3:24:57 Thanks very much.

3:24:59 So, uh, what I'd like to do is take the

3:25:01 parking fine increases, uh, question and

3:25:04 then take a break. So, why don't we go

3:25:07 through that?

3:25:08 Yeah, I'll be very quick. Um,

3:25:10 I thought I thought this would be a

3:25:11 quick one.

3:25:12 Yeah. Um the long and the short of it is

3:25:14 as part of the way of paying for the

3:25:18 increases in costs in the following

3:25:19 years without um raising property

3:25:23 without asking to raise property taxes

3:25:24 further. We are looking wherever we can

3:25:26 at increasing fees and fines

3:25:28 appropriately. We have not touched

3:25:30 parking fines since 2007. Um we're due.

3:25:34 Um other communities are higher than we

3:25:36 are now. Cambridge recently raised many

3:25:39 of its fees to $40. Boston has its at

3:25:42 $4. Somervilles are even higher. I will

3:25:44 say that Newton, our other neighbor, is

3:25:46 lower at 25. It's closer to where we

3:25:48 are. Um, but I think there's a strong

3:25:51 case to be made that regionally it's

3:25:53 appropriate to raise fines to $40. You

3:25:56 cannot exceed a $50 fine per um local uh

3:26:01 law. Um so that's why unless there is a

3:26:05 statutory exemption for it, your fees

3:26:08 and your fines cannot exceed $50. Um,

3:26:11 and there has to be an escalating cost

3:26:13 structure. You know, if someone doesn't

3:26:14 pay within a certain amount of time, it

3:26:15 has to go up by a certain amount. And

3:26:17 then when it's reported to the state,

3:26:19 when it's reported to the RMV, it has to

3:26:21 go up again. Um, so, um, at that junk,

3:26:25 so what I've structured here is a

3:26:27 proposal that for every fine that is not

3:26:29 currently at $40, that it be increased

3:26:32 to $40. that the payment that the late

3:26:35 payment before it is reported to the

3:26:37 state be increased to $55 and then when

3:26:40 it is reported out that the fine go up

3:26:42 to $70. Both of those numbers are the

3:26:45 statutory maximum that the select board

3:26:47 is allowed to set for a parking

3:26:49 violation. So um the way that you did

3:26:52 this in 20 in sorry in 2007 was that you

3:26:55 asked the transportation board to appine

3:26:57 on it first. They provided a

3:26:58 recommendation to the select board and

3:27:00 the select board voted on the ultimate

3:27:01 increase. Technically, I think under the

3:27:03 law, the transportation board could do

3:27:05 it themselves. But I think in the

3:27:07 interest of maintaining tradition um and

3:27:09 also the you know maintaining that you

3:27:12 are the ultimate policym body and the

3:27:14 transportation board does have statutory

3:27:16 authority but it is an advis it has an

3:27:18 advisory role to play as well. Having

3:27:20 both boards concur on a fine increase I

3:27:23 think is is important. So I would take

3:27:25 it to them first um with your permission

3:27:27 and then bring it back to you with their

3:27:29 recommendation.

3:27:30 Okay. Anyone disagree?

3:27:32 I have

3:27:33 uh ju just a question. Uh you referred

3:27:37 specifically to parking violations. Are

3:27:39 they looking at moving violations also?

3:27:41 No, it's just this is just parking

3:27:43 violations.

3:27:44 Okay.

3:27:45 Yeah. So, um Chaz, this is uniform

3:27:48 across the town.

3:27:50 Yes.

3:27:50 Okay. So, my question is are we are we

3:27:53 limited to that uniformed across the

3:27:56 town? The reason why I ask is I think um

3:27:59 the abuse of parking um is different by

3:28:03 region of the town. So for example in uh

3:28:07 in Beacon Street down by St. Mary's

3:28:10 because of red the Red Sox games and

3:28:12 often concerts.

3:28:14 Um it's heavily abused the parking and

3:28:17 it's cheaper to have to pay $40 than to

3:28:20 pay $65 uh you know by the park or it

3:28:24 you know closer into the the venue. Um

3:28:27 and I see this because people park and

3:28:30 they're happy to get the ticket.

3:28:32 Um can we can we make it more uh

3:28:35 location specific?

3:28:36 I don't know the answer to that. I will

3:28:38 ask town council's office. I have not

3:28:39 seen that done but that doesn't mean

3:28:41 that it can't be done. So let me ask and

3:28:42 I will if if that is the case then we'll

3:28:44 discuss that with the T board and bring

3:28:46 that back to you.

3:28:46 Okay, that'll be helpful. Thank you.

3:28:50 Uh John,

3:28:50 do we have any estimate estimate as to

3:28:53 how much u the current revenue is versus

3:28:56 how much the revenue would be with this

3:28:57 $40?

3:28:58 We estimate that we would bring in on

3:29:00 average about an additional $1.2 million

3:29:03 um just if everything stayed consistent.

3:29:05 We want to be very conservative when we

3:29:07 look at these for the first time. So, we

3:29:09 did a rolling five-year average and we

3:29:10 also assumed that in the event that fees

3:29:14 the fines go up, you do see some

3:29:15 increased compliance. So, that kicks it

3:29:17 down a little bit. So, ultimately, we

3:29:19 are projecting in FY27, if these fines

3:29:22 are approved, an increase in roughly

3:29:24 $1.06 million in local receipts.

3:29:28 That's impressive.

3:29:29 Yeah,

3:29:29 that's the hope.

3:29:30 Real money.

3:29:31 Um, that's the hope. You know, again,

3:29:33 this is a conservative projection, but

3:29:34 we also, you know,

3:29:35 we we want to be frank with people that,

3:29:37 you know, the the rate the the fines are

3:29:41 not taking we're still not taking in

3:29:42 what we used to take in before the

3:29:44 pandemic.

3:29:44 Um, we're still not where we used to be,

3:29:46 and that was when they were at a lower

3:29:48 rate.

3:29:49 So, um, you know, it's a combination of

3:29:52 fewer people on the road, you know, the

3:29:55 um,

3:29:56 you know, some of the issues with

3:29:57 enforcement um, and and you know, in

3:29:59 some cases compliance. Um so you know

3:30:02 one of the things that we want to do

3:30:03 with this is we want to take some of

3:30:05 this money and directly reinvest it in

3:30:07 our parking infrastructure so that we

3:30:10 have better systems in place. You know

3:30:13 we we for examp you know there are

3:30:16 tickets that get thrown out because

3:30:17 meters are broken for example like we

3:30:19 want to reinvest directly in the process

3:30:21 to make sure that there are fewer broken

3:30:22 meters and there's fewer excuses when

3:30:24 those things pop up. um we want to have

3:30:27 a more functional and cohesive parking

3:30:29 enforcement infrastructure. So, we're

3:30:31 going to take some of this money and

3:30:32 reinvest it directly into that and then

3:30:35 the rest of it will be run through the

3:30:36 town school split like it usually does.

3:30:39 I I mean I just add one other factor

3:30:40 that I hope is taken into consideration

3:30:43 because this does come at a cost. Um

3:30:46 there's been discussion recently about u

3:30:49 adjusting the rates for parking

3:30:51 depending on how close you are into the

3:30:54 heart of a commercial district like

3:30:55 Brooklyn Village versus parking at

3:30:58 outlying parking meters. Yes. Um, that's

3:31:00 all well and good, but once we, you

3:31:03 know, jack up the the amount of the

3:31:06 meter rate and then we add on that and,

3:31:09 you know, God help you, if if you

3:31:11 overpark by two minutes and you get a

3:31:14 $40 fine, eventually people are going to

3:31:17 say, I'm going to go somewhere where I'm

3:31:19 less likely to, you know, have to spend

3:31:21 a lot of money just because I parked at

3:31:23 a meter that was near the store and then

3:31:25 I, you know, made the mistake of staying

3:31:27 too long and next thing you know, a

3:31:29 simple shopping trip to Brooklyn Village

3:31:31 has cost me over, you know, $50, right?

3:31:33 Um, so you got to balance all of these

3:31:36 things, right?

3:31:36 That's right. And I will say I know that

3:31:38 a town meeting member came last week and

3:31:40 had a question, a good question I

3:31:41 thought about why don't we raise the

3:31:42 parking meter rates again. And the

3:31:43 answer is we did that last override.

3:31:45 Yeah. You know, we raised it, you know,

3:31:47 well aware

3:31:48 and so, you know, it's not hasn't been

3:31:50 very long since we've been at the $2

3:31:52 mark and we've seen, you know, people

3:31:54 avoiding the meters, people people

3:31:56 trying to park elsewhere because of

3:31:58 that. So, we have seen revenue from

3:32:01 parking meters actually start to become

3:32:03 more volatile as a result of the fact

3:32:05 that our rates are higher. I worry if we

3:32:07 push them higher, especially since we

3:32:09 just pushed them higher, you know, not

3:32:10 even five years ago, um that you will

3:32:13 start to see that sort of avoidance. Um,

3:32:16 and unlike here, unlike with the fines

3:32:20 where I think you have other

3:32:22 communities, not just Boston, at that

3:32:25 $40 mark or higher, um, no one except

3:32:28 Boston, as to my knowledge, is above the

3:32:30 $2 an hour um, rate um, for street

3:32:34 parking currently. Um, although again, I

3:32:37 haven't done the survey on that, but

3:32:39 it's my understanding based on

3:32:40 conversations I've had that Boston is

3:32:42 kind of the outlier there as opposed to

3:32:45 um where we are on this. So I I do want

3:32:48 people to be assured that we have looked

3:32:50 at that. But to your point, you know,

3:32:52 you want to be careful not to create a

3:32:54 situation that actually that

3:32:55 accidentally upsets the the balance that

3:32:58 you've created here.

3:32:59 Yeah. And and one other consideration

3:33:01 too is appeals processes. people are

3:33:04 going to be a m much more likely having

3:33:06 experienced a $40 ticket in Boston when

3:33:10 I went to get a slice of pizza. Um, and

3:33:13 it was not appropriately uh done because

3:33:16 the there was just a disagreement over

3:33:18 which meter was the one that my car was

3:33:21 parked at. Um, and I went through the

3:33:23 appeals process and it was um,

3:33:25 impressive and it came out just fine for

3:33:28 me, but I probably wouldn't have

3:33:30 appealed, you know, a $15, uh, penalty,

3:33:33 right? Um, and so all of these things

3:33:36 kind of interact, right?

3:33:37 Yeah.

3:33:38 And Michael,

3:33:38 uh, I'm just curious, following up on on

3:33:41 the parking question, um, whether

3:33:42 there's any evidence of the increase in

3:33:48 modes of transportation. I don't have an

3:33:51 answer on that. I'll talk to the

3:53:38 All right. Um so

3:53:41 the uh you receive the mailer uh and

3:53:44 about uh 400 people uh 300 people did

3:53:47 also receive that. We sent out an email

3:53:50 to the select board, the school

3:53:51 committee, advisory committee, town

3:53:53 meeting members. Um so they received my

3:53:55 memo uh Friday. We also put it out um on

3:53:59 the town's website. Um we also put it

3:54:02 through um the notify me or what's the

3:54:05 new vendor um for people who signed up

3:54:07 on the town meeting list serve. So that

3:54:09 was about 700 people. They got a notice

3:54:12 with the link to the memo. So that

3:54:13 describes the process. But what I wanted

3:54:15 to share with you is just kind of the

3:54:17 bullet point deadline and and the first

3:54:21 deadline is this Friday to receive

3:54:23 arguments. Advocates step forward and

3:54:26 draft 150 words. Um, you may say, "Oh my

3:54:29 god, that's a quick turnaround." But

3:54:31 hold on, we'll get to the bottom and

3:54:32 you'll see why. Uh, but this is supposed

3:54:35 to be a public process. So, last time

3:54:37 around, I shared publicly

3:54:40 the drafts and as we evolved from the

3:54:43 initial arguments to the final arguments

3:54:45 that is put before you for approval.

3:54:48 So, Tuesday after we receive the initial

3:54:51 uh drafts, they'll be fact checked.

3:54:52 they'll be um um you know um made sure

3:54:56 that we received them and I'll circulate

3:54:58 the draft of the ballot information

3:55:01 Tuesday, April 7th. This will give not

3:55:04 only the advocates but the public a

3:55:06 chance to respond and the advocates will

3:55:08 then have till Thursday the 9th to res

3:55:10 to to um amend their initial arguments

3:55:13 to then get a final copy to you on

3:55:15 Friday the 10th. Again, this is to you

3:55:18 and the public. Um, so then you can vote

3:55:21 on it two weeks from tonight. And again,

3:55:23 you may say, "Well, that's a, you know,

3:55:26 that's pretty fast." It is, but here's

3:55:29 why.

3:55:30 It's going to take uh town clerk staff

3:55:33 uh three days, basically, three eight

3:55:36 hour days to print, fold, stuff, seal,

3:55:40 and put postage on a 30,000

3:55:43 piece mailer. Um they're going to do

3:55:45 this after they do the vote by mail

3:55:48 ballots which will be three days

3:55:49 previously. So that will be the Monday,

3:55:52 Tuesday, Wednesday, the finishing on the

3:55:54 15th and ideally start this Thursday the

3:55:56 16th, 17th and then over the weekend. So

3:55:59 the plan is to get this mailer out

3:56:02 Monday, April 20th. That is a day the

3:56:05 post office is open, but it is Patriots

3:56:08 day, which we'll have a marathon running

3:56:10 through town, so it's not the best day,

3:56:12 but it will be the first day that we'll

3:56:14 chance, you know, we'll have a chance to

3:56:15 get this out, but that's only 15 days

3:56:18 before election day. Um, and it's only 5

3:56:21 days before we start inerson voting.

3:56:24 But it would be very difficult to make

3:56:26 that

3:56:27 period even more um because again, you

3:56:30 saw these quick deadlines. we're we're

3:56:32 getting the arguments in just a couple

3:56:35 days and then basically put back before

3:56:38 you for vote the following week. So,

3:56:40 it'd be really hard to have more time

3:56:43 for people to get this and I wouldn't

3:56:45 recommend stretching it out because I do

3:56:48 feel uncomfortable with all the problems

3:56:50 that we've had with the uh mail that

3:56:52 we're only allowing 15 days to get these

3:56:54 to people's um homes before they vote.

3:56:58 So, these are the deadlines. Again, as

3:57:00 Chaz said, we're looking for an advocate

3:57:01 in opposition, but otherwise, people

3:57:03 feel free to give me a call. Um, or

3:57:06 shoot me an email.

3:57:07 Uh, we're getting them and we're

3:57:08 responding, but if people want to send

3:57:10 more, feel free.
