3:58:32 Okay. Let's move on to the next item

3:58:34 which is the U 8mm warrant article

3:58:38 public hearing. Oh, it's 9:25. Well,

3:58:42 that's life in in Brooklyn. Um, okay.

3:58:46 First item is going to be article 20

3:58:49 resolution to establish an official

3:58:51 annual townwide commemoration of

3:58:54 September 11, 200 one. Um, typo in the

3:59:00 agenda. And and a memorial to all those

3:59:03 from Brooklyn, Massachusetts who died on

3:59:06 September 11, 2001. I see Elizabeth

3:59:10 Charles is at the microphone.

3:59:13 So,

3:59:14 say

3:59:16 thank you very much for opening up this

3:59:19 and I appreciate you for for hearing

3:59:21 about our article tonight, article uh

3:59:23 20, which is a resolution um about the

3:59:26 911 remembrance. Um I'm Elizabeth

3:59:29 Childs, town member uh precinct 4. And I

3:59:32 want to introduce our co-etitioners. We

3:59:34 have Ariel Guzman, town member precinct

3:59:37 five, and Jared Alexandro, um, who is a

3:59:40 Lawrence parent who just had to run home

3:59:42 and take his put his son to bed. Um, and

3:59:44 an 18-year, uh, Brooklyn resident who

3:59:46 was a senior at South High School in

3:59:48 Worcester when 911 happened.

3:59:51 We also have Nancy Okconor, who's not

3:59:53 able to be here tonight, who's a wife,

3:59:54 mother, and daughter in a

3:59:56 multi-generational firefighter family in

3:59:59 Brooklyn. and one of the firefighters

4:00:01 and their families who raised the money

4:00:03 for the Manny Del Valley bench at fire

4:00:05 station number five on Bcott Street.

4:00:07 These are the petitioners.

4:00:10 So, article 20 builds um on several pri

4:00:13 prior efforts to remember 911 in

4:00:16 Brooklyn and I am very grateful to many

4:00:19 of you personally, in particular to you,

4:00:20 Chair Green, for all of your support in

4:00:23 the past to create this event in

4:00:25 Brooklyn.

4:00:27 The resolution simply asks us to

4:00:29 formalize this annual remembrance

4:00:31 ceremony so that it is sustainable no

4:00:34 matter who's doing the organizing of the

4:00:37 citizen volunteers. And we modeled our

4:00:40 resolution on the resolution that the um

4:00:43 MLK committee did after they had had

4:00:45 their informal citizen group and then

4:00:47 became a formal committee so that it

4:00:49 would have sustainability in town.

4:00:53 The resolution asks the select board to

4:00:55 appoint a committee for two roles. The

4:00:58 first role is to do the leg work of

4:01:00 coordinating the logistics of the annual

4:01:03 ceremony. The second one is a time

4:01:05 related role which is to to um identify

4:01:10 and make recommendations to the select

4:01:12 board about a physical marker in

4:01:14 Brookline. And that that part of the

4:01:17 role for the committee would end once

4:01:19 they made the recommendation to the

4:01:20 select board. The article does not um

4:01:23 propose how that physical marker would

4:01:25 be, where it would be, but it actually

4:01:27 asked the committee to do that work.

4:01:32 The importance of 911 cannot be

4:01:34 overstated, particularly to our

4:01:36 community. Brookline is one of the

4:01:39 communities that lost more people on 911

4:01:42 than any other community saved New York

4:01:44 City and Boston. In fact, we may be the

4:01:47 community that lost the most out beyond

4:01:50 New York City and Boston. These are

4:01:52 citizens who are on their way to work,

4:01:55 who committed acts of valor that day. In

4:01:58 fact, the first person to die on 911 was

4:02:01 a Brooklyn resident who lived not far

4:02:03 from me, Daniel Leuen. And he um heard

4:02:07 and understood the people behind him

4:02:09 speaking Arabic. And we believe that he

4:02:11 tried to stop them from crashing the

4:02:14 first plane into the north tower.

4:02:17 We also know that some of the first

4:02:19 responders who died that day and many

4:02:20 who were ill um had Brooklyn

4:02:23 connections, at least one of them died

4:02:24 in the line of service, uh Mannyell

4:02:27 Valley. We also know that um the brother

4:02:29 of Jack Dewan, who was a longtime

4:02:31 Brooklyn firefighter, also died in the

4:02:33 line of service that day. These are

4:02:35 ordinary people, people doing their job

4:02:37 and people who had no idea that that day

4:02:40 when they went to work, they were never

4:02:41 going to come home.

4:02:44 So the committee has proposed is really

4:02:46 a way of us in Brooklyn taking a moment

4:02:49 to come together to remember what 911

4:02:53 meant to our country and what it meant

4:02:54 in particular to Brooklyn.

4:02:58 Um

4:03:00 I I think there's one note I want to

4:03:02 just make as a like a practical note.

4:03:04 There's a scrimer's error in the article

4:03:07 which states that the number of

4:03:09 casualties was 2,997.

4:03:12 It actually is 2977.

4:03:16 So that n that second nine should be a

4:03:18 seven.

4:03:19 And the reason I raise this is because

4:03:20 we can't change it in the warrant. But

4:03:23 if the select board decides to move the

4:03:25 article, I would want us to change it in

4:03:27 that motion. If the AC decides to move

4:03:30 it, I would have them change it. And if

4:03:32 neither the AC or the select board move

4:03:33 it and we have to move it on the floor,

4:03:35 we will make that amendment at that

4:03:37 time. But I didn't want there to be any

4:03:39 we didn't want any inaccuracies in our

4:03:42 article. Um,

4:03:45 that's actually all I have to add to

4:03:46 say. I can I'm certainly happy to answer

4:03:48 questions from all of you and um and I

4:03:51 just want to ask my fellow petitioners

4:03:52 if there's anything else they need to

4:03:54 add to that explanation.

4:03:57 Jared, yes, please. Jared Alexandro.

4:04:01 Thank Thank you, Elizabeth. Um some of

4:04:04 you saw that I was here tonight with my

4:04:06 10-year-old son who's a student at

4:04:07 Lawrence. And I think part of this is

4:04:10 really you're seeing the impact that

4:04:12 911's had being generation to

4:04:15 generation. And we don't want um the

4:04:17 students that are coming up in Brooklyn

4:04:18 to not have the appreciation for our

4:04:21 first responders and what that meant to

4:04:24 people of you know I was in high school

4:04:26 so very clear day in my mind but it's

4:04:29 not something that's talked about on a

4:04:31 daily basis like it once was. So we

4:04:33 think it's really important for the new

4:04:35 generation of Brooklyn students and

4:04:37 residents coming up that there be a

4:04:39 permanent remembrance um to honor the

4:04:41 the victims of that day. Thank you.

4:04:45 Ariel, anything you want to add? Okay, I

4:04:47 think that's that's all we have to say.

4:04:49 We're happy to answer questions.

4:04:51 Okay. Um, I have a couple comments. Yes.

4:05:07 No, it is nationally recognized as

4:05:09 Patriot Patriot Day.

4:05:11 Patriot Day.

4:05:12 Okay. National Day of Service and

4:05:14 Remembrance. Yes.

4:05:15 So just distinguish from our Patriots.

4:05:18 Exactly. It's ours is Patriots Day.

4:05:21 Uh secondly, I I would recommend that um

4:05:25 in the first uh now therefore clause um

4:05:30 first whereas clause. Okay.

4:05:32 No, now therefore clause.

4:05:33 Oh, in the first now therefore. Okay.

4:05:36 Um you know this is the 250th

4:05:38 anniversary of uh our revolution.

4:05:41 Yes. Uh, so to say that this is one of

4:05:43 the most important days in American

4:05:46 history, I think is probably not what

4:05:48 you want to say.

4:05:51 Okay.

4:05:52 Okay. That's just my opinion.

4:05:53 Okay.

4:05:54 Um, I mean, you know, we have Pearl

4:05:56 Harbor, we have a lot of, you know,

4:05:58 important days and maybe different

4:06:01 phrasing there. The the other thing that

4:06:04 I would like to suggest is that it looks

4:06:07 like, as I recall when I read this, it

4:06:10 looks like uh the committee will

4:06:13 recommend to the select board that we

4:06:16 commission and uh I guess fund a

4:06:19 memorial. Shouldn't that be a

4:06:21 fundraising um activity of a committee?

4:06:24 Well, I think it could be. I I think we

4:06:26 don't presuppose that that wouldn't be

4:06:28 the case. I mean, I think the committee

4:06:30 uh could make recommendations even

4:06:31 around how those funds would be raised.

4:06:33 Yes. And the committee we proposed would

4:06:36 be appointed by the select board and

4:06:38 have the committee we proposed had the

4:06:40 five members, five public members, um a

4:06:44 member designated by the fire chief, a

4:06:45 member designated by the police chief, a

4:06:48 member designated by the Brooklyn

4:06:49 Historical Society, and a select board

4:06:51 member, obviously. Yeah.

4:06:54 Yeah.

4:06:55 Any other questions?

4:06:58 Go ahead.

4:06:58 Yes.

4:06:59 So, so thank you very much um for the

4:07:02 presentation and for all the efforts um

4:07:05 you've put into this remembrance. Um

4:07:08 I I I am interested in noting the fact

4:07:12 that you brought up Pearl Harbor Day. Um

4:07:14 because when I was sort of thinking

4:07:17 about the the analogies in our history,

4:07:19 that was that was the one that came to

4:07:22 mind. And I

4:07:26 one of the reasons is that my one of my

4:07:29 uncles was actually born on December

4:07:31 7th, couple even years later. Um so that

4:07:34 day has always sort of stuck with me for

4:07:36 that reason. Um but but I'm just I'm

4:07:40 just wondering, you know, when I think

4:07:42 about sort of what our current uh

4:07:45 discussions are about that day now, you

4:07:49 know, some 80 80 some odd years later.

4:07:52 I'm trying to think about what are your

4:07:55 expectations of how this would evolve 80

4:07:58 some odd years after 2001.

4:08:01 Yeah. Well, I think we don't know what

4:08:03 what will happen, but I would hope that

4:08:05 we' we would remember it just like I

4:08:07 think this is a generational thing, too,

4:08:09 that um people of my era and my parents'

4:08:13 era always remembered and stopped on

4:08:16 Pearl Harbor Day,

4:08:18 but our younger generation in a way um

4:08:22 to 911 was really like their Pearl

4:08:24 Harbor. And actually more people were

4:08:26 killed on 911 than on Pearl Harbor. And

4:08:29 um in fact it is the single greatest

4:08:32 number of Americans killed on by a

4:08:35 foreign adversary in America on in a day

4:08:39 and it's the largest terrorist attack in

4:08:41 the history of the world. Um so it is a

4:08:43 very significant day and I think that I

4:08:45 would hope that we would just take a

4:08:46 pause to all come together and we

4:08:49 remember our humanity and um I hope that

4:08:53 they do do it 50 years from now that

4:08:54 somebody will stop and say today is 911.

4:08:57 We should remember those people and I

4:08:59 think we at least we do in our household

4:09:02 on on Pearl Harbor Day, but I don't know

4:09:05 if that happens anywhere else, you know,

4:09:07 and publicly. I don't know that we do

4:09:08 anything like that in in Brooklyn. I

4:09:10 don't know that we do. Um so I hope it I

4:09:13 hope we would keep doing this at least.

4:09:15 Yeah.

4:09:17 Um thank you for bringing this forward.

4:09:19 Can you describe what it takes today to

4:09:21 put this on? Who who's doing the work?

4:09:24 Um I know I see you there. Right. See

4:09:26 Bernard there.

4:09:27 But my sense is it it it seems to be

4:09:31 puddled together, bootstrapped.

4:09:32 It is it is it's it's actually wonderful

4:09:35 effort. It really Bernard really

4:09:37 deserves a lot of credit here because um

4:09:40 uh Susan Howard and I approached Bernard

4:09:42 about doing something on in Brooklyn on

4:09:44 911 because although there was the

4:09:46 fireman's solemn ceremony which they

4:09:48 will have and always have, it's a very

4:09:50 it's a very important day for them as

4:09:51 well. Um we felt that it was really

4:09:54 important for our community like all the

4:09:56 communities around us actually were

4:09:57 doing things and we were like not doing

4:09:59 a citizen effort but a lot of the most

4:10:02 the people who died on that day were

4:10:04 ordinary citizens like you and me who

4:10:05 just got up and went to work ended up

4:10:08 being confronted with doing something

4:10:09 incredible. Um and many of them died in

4:10:13 the line of doing that. Um, and so

4:10:16 Bernard actually really led the ar led

4:10:18 the push to make sure that we linked up

4:10:22 with the fireman's solemn ceremony

4:10:24 because we didn't want to compete with

4:10:25 it. We really wanted to just if anything

4:10:28 just augment it but remember the

4:10:30 citizens who also died that day besides

4:10:32 the firefighters and the police officers

4:10:34 who died that day. And so the most

4:10:35 important thing we do on that day is to

4:10:37 read the names of the people from

4:10:39 Brooklyn who died on 911. And when when

4:10:42 David had to pinch it and he did a great

4:10:44 job last year on 911, the most important

4:10:47 thing you have to do, I remember saying

4:10:48 to him, was to read the names. Um, and

4:10:51 so it really is trying to make

4:10:53 sustainable

4:10:55 an effort. I'm not thinking that those

4:10:56 of us who have worked on it are happy to

4:10:58 continue to work on it. We really don't

4:11:00 mind doing the work, but we'd love to

4:11:03 have it be on the town calendar. We'd

4:11:05 love to have it be something that gets

4:11:07 shared across town is like this is 911

4:11:09 and and we want you all to come by and

4:11:12 take 30 minutes out of your morning and

4:11:15 and uh share with us what happened that

4:11:17 day.

4:11:18 So just to follow up so um just two two

4:11:22 quick two quick points, one personal and

4:11:24 then another which I do to talk about

4:11:27 the impact of this. Um I actually knew

4:11:29 uh Daniel Ruin. Um and I knew another

4:11:32 individual, Ando Curry Green, who was

4:11:34 sitting either directly behind Daniel or

4:11:36 the seat the next row.

4:11:37 Yeah.

4:11:38 Um and they did not know each other, but

4:11:39 I knew both of them very well.

4:11:41 Um and um so it is a very moving time

4:11:45 for me when whenever the anniversary

4:11:47 comes that those are the folks I think

4:11:48 of as well as all the others. But if you

4:11:50 think about something that impacted us

4:11:53 um you know 911

4:11:57 uh dramatically changed our society and

4:12:01 and not only the US but globally.

4:12:05 Yes.

4:12:05 Um and to this day we take our well I

4:12:08 don't think we have to take our shoes

4:12:09 off anymore but you certainly have to

4:12:10 take our belts off and go through

4:12:11 scanners and all that. That's a direct

4:12:13 result.

4:12:13 Yes.

4:12:14 Of what occurred on 911. Yes. um you

4:12:16 know the the the government's invasion

4:12:18 of our privacy frankly and ability to

4:12:21 monitor and

4:12:22 we don't even know what they can do now

4:12:24 as a result of of 911.

4:12:27 Um and so it's its impact

4:12:30 um even if we're not having a ceremony

4:12:35 is felt today. Yeah.

4:12:37 Um in in our daily lives I think because

4:12:40 of that. So, I do think it's uh it's

4:12:42 worthy of um serious consideration to

4:12:44 formalize the ceremony and I really

4:12:46 appreciate you bringing this forward.

4:12:48 Thank you. Yeah, I actually in our

4:12:49 petitioner's explanation, I listed some

4:12:51 of those things that you're mentioning,

4:12:53 Paul, that it changed us dramatically.

4:12:55 It created the Department of Homeland

4:12:57 Security. Um it the bringing down of the

4:13:01 Twin Towers was bringing down the

4:13:02 international symbol of the United

4:13:04 States economic power. The invasion of

4:13:06 Afghanistan occurred on October 7th,

4:13:08 2001 as a direct result.

4:13:11 Um, and it it it made Americans realize

4:13:15 that we were hated in places in the

4:13:17 world and that we were vulnerable. And

4:13:19 personally, there are still people I

4:13:21 treat some who actually witnessed

4:13:23 firsthand as children the tra the

4:13:26 tragedy and the death on that day and

4:13:29 they are continually haunted by those

4:13:31 traumatic memories. So it is a very very

4:13:33 important day for many people. Yeah.

4:13:36 Thank you.

4:13:37 Yeah. I think it's particularly

4:13:39 important for us to have a memorial with

4:13:42 the names of Brooklyn connected people

4:13:44 and that that's an extremely powerful

4:13:48 um thing to do that that uh you know

4:13:52 succeeding generations can can look at

4:13:55 and think about and and try to

4:13:57 understand you what happened and why it

4:13:59 happened. uh and um our connection to

4:14:03 that.

4:14:03 Yeah.

4:14:06 Any other discussion? This is a public

4:14:09 hearing, so let's open up the public

4:14:11 hearing. I see someone raising their

4:14:13 hands.

4:14:20 Regina Frolley uh from South Brooklyn

4:14:22 and I attend as some of you know every

4:14:24 one of these uh honorable and honoring

4:14:28 occasions including the Babcock Street.

4:14:33 In 1976, I took a seminar at Harvard on

4:14:37 Vietnam War. And there was myself, a

4:14:40 Vietnam vet, and several young people

4:14:45 collecting credits, I guess, for their

4:14:47 degree.

4:14:50 In one, it was the year after the the um

4:14:55 soldiers were taken out of Vietnam,

4:14:58 evacuated. Uh, and we know, we've never

4:15:01 forgotten what that looked like. In that

4:15:03 room, the children had zero,

4:15:06 they were privileged children. They had

4:15:08 zero connection. They probably didn't

4:15:10 have anyone in their family that was

4:15:12 served in Vietnam.

4:15:15 They thought it was just a history

4:15:17 course and they were not very engaged.

4:15:20 That stuck with me one year after the

4:15:23 evacuation. And that's the indifference

4:15:26 I saw in the children. And I'm not

4:15:28 criticizing them. I'm saying that we

4:15:31 fail them when we don't educate them. I

4:15:34 feel that 911 is everything that

4:15:36 Elizabeth has said and more. We have a

4:15:40 generation of children whose families

4:15:42 have not had the the draft, have not

4:15:46 served in the military, have in fact had

4:15:49 very good lives. And we need to honor

4:15:54 these occasions. they did have all the

4:15:56 consequences that Elizabeth and and

4:15:59 others have mentioned and I think it's

4:16:01 really important as an education for the

4:16:04 children to do this. Uh I agree with the

4:16:07 father enormously about that. Um we

4:16:11 can't raise our children in bubbles as

4:16:13 if nothing happens in life. We are

4:16:16 hated. We probably more now than ever.

4:16:19 Um, we need to to have the children be

4:16:22 aware of what it is to serve.

4:16:25 And the best part about the annual event

4:16:29 at Babco Street is this is not about the

4:16:32 enemy. It has never been about the

4:16:35 enemy. It has been about the heroes and

4:16:37 the victims. And that's all they ever do

4:16:40 is just honor the memory of Americans

4:16:43 killed on their soil when they went to

4:16:45 work one day and the others who came to

4:16:48 rescue and all of so many of them have

4:16:51 still got health issues if they haven't

4:16:53 already died. So I hope this will be

4:16:56 taken seriously because I've seen that

4:16:59 one year after the evacuation that there

4:17:01 were whole number of children who had no

4:17:05 idea young people who had no idea of the

4:17:08 consequences of Vietnam and if you

4:17:10 Santiana said if you don't understand

4:17:12 history you will be condemned to repeat

4:17:15 it. Thank you

4:17:18 David.

4:17:20 So I also want to thank the petitioners

4:17:22 for bringing this forward. I I agree

4:17:24 this is uh critically important and

4:17:26 something that uh I mean I'm prepared to

4:17:29 support it now.

4:17:30 Uh I also like the idea that Paul raised

4:17:33 and of course it was it was already in

4:17:35 the language as uh you pointed out

4:17:37 Elizabeth on the penultimate page about

4:17:39 the uh significant impacts that this

4:17:42 date had. So it's I mean honoring those

4:17:46 who whose lives were lost is absolutely

4:17:49 critical. But I also like the idea of

4:17:50 coupling that with the broader cultural

4:17:54 global significance that it had uh on

4:17:57 all of our lives for those of us who are

4:17:59 alive now and those who weren't alive

4:18:02 yet at the time to understand that what

4:18:04 they are experiencing today in some

4:18:07 respects uh was born as a part of the

4:18:11 tragedy on that date. I'm curious uh to

4:18:14 what extent you and the other

4:18:16 petitioners research what other

4:18:19 communities do with this day. I I do

4:18:21 notice on the first page uh you

4:18:23 mentioned uh several Boston area

4:18:26 municipalities that have similar

4:18:28 committees and I presume that the New

4:18:30 York City area has municipalities. Uh do

4:18:33 they do anything different or were you

4:18:36 trying to model this after any community

4:18:38 in particular? We started with Newton

4:18:41 and um Newton has a ve a very elaborate

4:18:44 a big ceremony and they have a physical

4:18:46 marker. We're hoping to have some

4:18:47 physical marker. Doesn't have to be like

4:18:48 Newton's but um a physical marker and

4:18:50 they do their ceremony around that

4:18:52 physical marker. Um and yes, we know the

4:18:55 organizers in Newton and um uh got a lot

4:18:58 of information when we started our we

4:19:00 started bootstrapping coppbling our

4:19:02 little thing together. It was based on

4:19:03 what they had done. Um but and actually

4:19:07 Boston does quite a commemoration as

4:19:09 well because of how many people died

4:19:11 from Boston and New York always does

4:19:13 something at down at ground zero. Um and

4:19:17 uh some of our uh family members have

4:19:19 often have either come to Brooklyn or

4:19:21 they've gone down to New York. So um

4:19:24 we've been in touch with several of the

4:19:26 family members. Um, one of the family

4:19:28 members uh uh taped a um a a really

4:19:33 talking about her brother uh Jerry Dewan

4:19:36 who died and um we've had uh members

4:19:38 come to our ceremony here and um that's

4:19:41 probably a little something more more of

4:19:43 our of our town. It's like a little more

4:19:45 like we're a little more

4:19:47 we try to make it more about like the

4:19:49 people and the families and make it a

4:19:51 little more uh community oriented I

4:19:54 guess would be the way that that's

4:19:55 probably the one difference. But most

4:19:57 there are many communities most all the

4:19:59 communities around us have something um

4:20:02 formal for 9/11. Um we're the outlier

4:20:05 actually in that. Yeah.

4:20:09 John,

4:20:10 just a couple of things to mention. Um

4:20:12 and and uh you know, one is that uh

4:20:16 Brooklyn has a way of even in the

4:20:19 absence of the officially sanctioned um

4:20:22 uh ceremony which very appropriately

4:20:24 this warrant article uh calls for um

4:20:28 people rise up and Elizabeth was one of

4:20:32 them. Susan Howard was another of them.

4:20:34 Um our chair was another of them. um a

4:20:37 and they create, you know, out of

4:20:40 nothing an observance um that is very

4:20:44 heartfelt. You know, I've I've attend

4:20:46 I've been privileged to attend it many

4:20:48 many years

4:20:49 and um it's always a special day and I

4:20:52 always feel if anything the only thing

4:20:55 missing is more people, you know, if we

4:20:57 could just get more people. Yeah. One of

4:20:59 the things that um uh Elizabeth pointed

4:21:02 out to me um during the break uh is that

4:21:06 among other reasons to do this as an

4:21:08 official u uh committee and a function

4:21:11 of the town um the the ceremony that uh

4:21:15 has been carried on for so many years

4:21:17 now never got on the town calendar, you

4:21:20 know, which is too bad. I mean, and

4:21:22 there's reasons for that, but uh one of

4:21:25 the harder things to do to accomplish

4:21:27 was simply to let people know we had

4:21:30 this observance on September 11th.

4:21:33 Um and we had people participating who

4:21:36 had lost, you know, people in their

4:21:38 family on September 11th. Um, and I'm

4:21:41 just going to mention because I I always

4:21:43 like to sort of make people aware of a

4:21:46 wonderful poem that was written um that

4:21:48 captures the uh what is so special about

4:21:52 that day uh because there's going to be

4:21:55 a tendency on on the part of some people

4:21:57 to think of it as a political thing. It

4:21:59 isn't a political thing. It's all it's

4:22:01 all about discovering that that we had

4:22:05 within ourselves the capacity to be

4:22:07 heroic that you know the capacity to be

4:22:10 self-sacrificing and the poem is called

4:22:13 survivors found. It's by a poem poet by

4:22:16 the name of Joan Murray. It got read o

4:22:19 over the air on NPR and became sort of a

4:22:22 celebrated. Uh it expressed for a lot of

4:22:24 people what what September 11th meant to

4:22:28 them especially in the immediate

4:22:30 aftermath

4:22:31 and it sort of resonates to this day. So

4:22:33 if people want to read a poem that is

4:22:35 very effective in that regard, Survivors

4:22:38 Found by Joan Murray, it's excellent.

4:22:40 That's great. Thank you.

4:25:18 Okay. Chi-Chi Woo, town meeting member,

4:25:20 precinct 7, uh, a petitioner on article

4:25:24 17, one of two, um, artic articles this

4:25:28 evening dealing with civil immigration

4:25:30 enforcement activities. Um, the other

4:25:32 petitioners are Alec Leoitz and Jessica

4:25:35 Kiko, who will be talking about article

4:25:37 25. uh Marissa Babot is here today and

4:25:41 then Amy Newell and Scottenian who could

4:25:43 not join us. Um

4:25:46 so um this article um uh you know I

4:25:51 don't need to belabor too much how um

4:25:54 difficult it has been uh to be both an

4:25:58 immigrant or to just watch um what's

4:26:00 been happening with uh immigrants in

4:26:02 this country. Um so just a little

4:26:04 background um onethird of Brooklyn

4:26:06 residents are immigrants. We have the

4:26:08 highest percentage of foreignb born

4:26:10 residents after Everett according to the

4:26:12 Brooklyn Community Foundation. Um I also

4:26:17 don't need to belabor that um we have

4:26:19 seen some terrible atrocities by the

4:26:21 immigration and customs enforcement um

4:26:24 across the country. We witnesses surges

4:26:27 in Chicago, Minneapolis, and Portland,

4:26:30 Maine that have terrorized the

4:26:31 populations, both immigrants, US

4:26:33 citizens. We've seen the the murders of

4:26:36 Alex Prey and Renee Good. Um, and here

4:26:39 in Massachusetts, we have not had a

4:26:42 surge, but we've seen numerous instances

4:26:44 of unfair detainment by ICE. Um, such as

4:26:48 most notably the detainment of a TUS PhD

4:26:51 student for exercising her first

4:26:52 amendment rights. Um, uh, lots of other

4:26:56 Massachusetts residents detained mo vast

4:26:59 majority without criminal records. you

4:27:01 know, a Babson student who is detained

4:27:03 um contrary to court orders. Um

4:27:05 teenagers detained um and that they've

4:27:08 been detained in terrible facilities um

4:27:11 that are overcrowded um and that have uh

4:27:14 poor food um and poor sanitary

4:27:18 conditions and also numerous children.

4:27:20 And of course, we are all worried about

4:27:22 a surge by ICE in the Boston area given

4:27:25 um that blue cities tend to be target of

4:27:29 this administration.

4:27:32 Um so um we are bringing this warrant

4:27:34 article uh modeled on several uh other

4:27:38 um pieces of uh several other things

4:27:41 that have been recently issued. Um, most

4:27:43 notably the you, the select board,

4:27:45 issued a proclamation on February 10th,

4:27:47 2026 about no immigration enforcement on

4:27:51 town property. Similarly, the governor

4:27:54 issued a an executive order um regarding

4:27:57 stateowned property and Boston, Newton,

4:27:59 Cambridge, Somerville, and Chelsea and

4:28:01 Lynn have uh issued similar executive

4:28:04 orders. So, this particular warrant

4:28:07 article um is very similar to the

4:28:10 proclamation that the select board

4:28:12 issued on February 10th and we very much

4:28:15 appreciate that proclamation um as well

4:28:18 as the other actions both the select

4:28:20 board and the police department have

4:28:22 taken with respect to not cooperating

4:28:24 with ICE. So, the right now the Warren

4:28:26 article is two paragraphs. The first

4:28:29 paragraph is very very similar to the

4:28:30 first paragraph of the proclamation. The

4:28:32 biggest difference is we explicitly

4:28:35 named the police station as a town

4:28:37 facility that should not be used as a

4:28:39 staging area or processing location um

4:28:41 for immigration activities without a

4:28:43 judicial warrant. Um that's actually not

4:28:45 a substantive change because the police

4:28:47 station is just an example. It is of

4:28:50 course by nature a town facility. So it

4:28:52 was covered under under your

4:28:53 proclamation.

4:28:56 The second um

4:28:59 uh the second paragraph um

4:29:03 sorry my PowerPoint's not um is also

4:29:06 similar to the second paragraph of the

4:29:08 proclamation. Um now this afternoon we

4:29:12 got an email from town council

4:29:14 expressing concerns about the second uh

4:29:19 second sentence of the second paragraph.

4:29:22 Um even though it's the same text as was

4:29:24 in the proclamation for some reason

4:29:26 because it is going to be a Warren

4:29:28 article um that seemed to cause some

4:29:32 concerns. Um town council did suggest

4:29:35 some options for changing it um

4:29:37 including making it closer to what

4:29:39 Cambridge or Somerville has in their exe

4:29:41 executive orders. So, um, we here we

4:29:45 have some potential language that we

4:29:46 could tweak to the second sentence to

4:29:50 try to address the concerns of town

4:29:52 council. Um,

4:29:56 and then, um, you might be thinking,

4:29:59 well, why did you bring this as a bylaw

4:30:02 when we already issued our proclamation?

4:30:04 Um and this was triggered really um

4:30:07 because we watched the discussion of the

4:30:10 proclamation which you know obviously

4:30:11 very much supportive and when it came to

4:30:14 the discussion of how it would be

4:30:15 enforced you know we noted that one of

4:30:17 the speakers said well um we would

4:30:19 enforce it by going to court to seek an

4:30:21 injunction to enforce the ordinance and

4:30:24 the thought occurred well a proclamation

4:30:26 really isn't an ordinance but what is an

4:30:29 ordinance a bylaw is an ordinance and a

4:30:31 bylaw makes um this more enforceable. It

4:30:35 gives the it the force of a town

4:30:37 ordinance. It makes it permanent within

4:30:39 our bylaws. Um also um you know one one

4:30:43 concern we got was well you know this

4:30:45 codifies it. What if we want to change

4:30:47 our minds? Well first of all I hope we

4:30:49 never do change our minds that our

4:30:51 values will stay the same in terms of

4:30:53 not cooperating with civil immigration

4:30:55 enforcement. But if it um does change,

4:30:58 you know, town meetings should have a

4:31:00 say in that as legislative body for

4:31:03 Brooklyn, both in terms of making this

4:31:04 permanent and if a reversal is sought.

4:31:08 Um finally, um we did have a

4:31:12 conversation with the police department.

4:31:15 We had it after we submitted it this as

4:31:17 a warrant article. Um so this did not

4:31:20 make it to the version that is in the

4:31:22 warrant. Um the police department

4:31:24 expressed some concerns about

4:31:25 enforcement of this bylaw and that they

4:31:27 were concerned, you know, that they

4:31:29 didn't want police officers pressured

4:31:31 into arresting ICE officers or getting

4:31:33 into conflict with ICE officers um or

4:31:36 having some sort of uh issue um um where

4:31:40 uh where a police officer was only aware

4:31:43 of the bylaw and not the proclamation.

4:31:45 um you know they pointed to paragraph

4:31:48 three of the proclamation of something

4:31:49 very important that the enforcement is

4:31:51 reporting to the time administrator and

4:31:53 seeking uh an injunction. Um and so we

4:31:57 are willing you know we are more than

4:31:58 happy to add paragraph 3 to the warrant

4:32:02 article to the bylaw. We have consulted

4:32:04 with the moderator um and the moderator

4:32:06 said that this would be considered in

4:32:08 scope. Um, so we would be willing to

4:32:11 move a motion that includes paragraph

4:32:13 three and that um that changes the

4:32:15 second sentence of the second paragraph

4:32:17 per um the text that I showed you

4:32:20 earlier and happy to answer any

4:32:21 questions at this point.

4:32:25 By the way, the uh duty to intervene and

4:32:28 report is part of the post um uh law

4:32:35 with respect to law enforcement

4:32:38 officers. So there's some ambiguity as

4:32:40 whether that covers federal officer, but

4:32:43 that that language is in the post

4:32:45 statute or regulations.

4:32:49 so this may be a question for uh town

4:48:19 is is is too specific in that regard.

4:48:31 well with respect to that the the latter

4:48:34 point um uh I mean we were focused on

4:52:17 David,

4:52:17 so

4:52:19 just g getting back to the uh previous

4:52:22 point. So I agree with Michael. I I

4:52:35 it's not that we are necessarily against

4:52:46 while there. And I do appreciate that

4:52:49 the petitioners were basically modeling

4:52:51 this off of our proclamation. So I think

4:53:29 first sentence. And again this isn't a

4:53:34 think a mistake that we made in the

4:53:36 proclamation as well.

5:15:08 Excellent. Um so, good evening everyone.

5:15:10 Alec Levitz, town meeting member,

5:15:12 precinct date and member of the advisory

5:15:14 committee. Um speaking here on behalf of

5:15:16 myself and the other co-etitioners of

5:15:18 Warrant Article 25. Um Warren article 25

5:15:22 um is the companion article to 17 which

5:15:25 where the approach in 17 was essentially

5:15:28 identify what we can do locally we think

5:15:30 by bylaw. Uh, Warren Article 25 takes a

5:15:34 broader view of additional policy

5:15:36 changes that we believe um would

5:15:39 strengthen Brookline and the state's uh

5:15:42 commitment to no cooperation with ICE,

5:15:44 which unfortunately in the uncharted

5:15:46 territory we are in, we feel must be a

5:15:49 policy priority for the town um given

5:15:52 the rogue direction the agency has gone

5:15:54 in and the um loss of human life that

5:15:56 they've inflicted without need or cause.

5:15:59 Um, so I I'll briefly cover um some of

5:16:02 the resolutions provisions that are

5:16:04 specifically directed at state officials

5:16:07 and then I'll pass it over to Jessica

5:16:09 who's going to talk more about the local

5:16:11 facing provisions of the resolution. Um

5:16:14 on the state side um there's three main

5:16:17 requests included in the resolution um

5:16:21 or provisions I should say. One is um a

5:16:24 condemnation of the actions taken by the

5:16:26 Trump administration um and federal

5:16:29 officials in their targeting in the con

5:16:32 the conducting of federal immigration

5:16:34 enforcement um of targeted enforcement

5:16:38 based on perceived race, ethnicity,

5:16:40 language or political preferences of

5:16:43 residents in the country. Um this is

5:16:46 something that's been unfortunately very

5:16:47 well documented in legal filings from

5:16:50 the Trump administration. um and seems

5:16:52 to be a commitment of their immigration

5:16:54 program. We find that completely

5:16:56 reprehensible and unconstitutional and

5:16:59 certainly not in keeping with the values

5:17:00 of Brooklyn under the United States. Um

5:17:03 focused at the Healey administration, um

5:17:06 this resolution requests that the state

5:17:08 exit the sole remaining 287g agreement

5:17:13 held by a New England state which is

5:17:15 held by the state's Department of

5:17:17 Corrections. This is the official

5:17:19 cooperation agreement provided under by

5:17:22 by federal law by which state um

5:17:25 entities can formally cooperate with

5:17:27 ISIS activities. Um in this case,

5:17:30 Department of Corrections um officially

5:17:32 participating in federal immigration

5:17:34 enforcement. Uh again given um the fact

5:17:37 that death and injury in ICE captivity

5:17:41 has risen precipitously uh and their

5:17:44 behavior in the pursuit of the Trump

5:17:45 administration's um goals on

5:17:47 immigration. We just feel this is

5:17:48 something that the state can no longer

5:17:50 countenance um and can no longer in good

5:17:52 conscience provide transfer of anyone uh

5:17:55 to ICE custody for fear of their their

5:17:57 personal safety and violation of their

5:17:59 rights. Um and finally, we also make a

5:18:02 request of the state legislature, which

5:18:04 is we call on them uh and particularly

5:18:06 Brooklyn's delegation to advance

5:18:08 legislation um um substantively similar

5:18:13 to um your general order 43 um that

5:18:17 seeks to um prevent or prohibit any

5:18:20 cooperation and further cooperation

5:18:22 between both state and local law

5:18:24 enforcement and federal immigration. Um,

5:18:27 and with that, I'll hand it over to

5:18:28 Jessica to talk about some of the local

5:18:29 facing provisions.

5:18:31 Thank you, Alec. Thank you, everyone.

5:18:33 Um, Jessica Kiko once again. I'm the

5:18:35 chair of the immigrant advancement

5:18:36 committee of the CDICR,

5:18:39 uh, where I've served since 2018. My day

5:18:41 job is as an immigration lawyer and

5:18:44 director of training at the

5:18:45 Massachusetts Immigrant Refugee Advocacy

5:18:47 Coalition, Mera, though I'm here in my

5:18:49 personal capacity, 14-year Brooklyn

5:18:52 resident, parent of BHS um, kids. So, in

5:18:56 addition to what was just presented, the

5:18:58 resolution calls on the town to apply

5:19:00 general order 51 uh for peaceful

5:19:02 demonstrations and legal observers to

5:19:04 ICE actions and to investigate excessive

5:19:07 use of force by federal officials under

5:19:09 general uh order 50. To withdraw from

5:19:12 the Boston Regional Information Center

5:19:14 or brick. uh to stop sending

5:19:16 fingerprints to the FBI for misdemeanors

5:19:19 and for minors and to stop applying for

5:19:22 grants under the urban area security

5:19:24 initiative or boyc program. And I'm

5:19:27 going to say a little bit more about

5:19:28 each next slide. Um so when we spoke

5:19:32 with our police department just last

5:19:34 week including Chief Pastor and

5:19:36 Superintendent Campbell who's here

5:19:37 tonight, they highlighted for us the

5:19:39 value of the shared resources that

5:19:41 Brookline has access to through the

5:19:42 brick. But the brick is not just about

5:19:45 sharing resources. It's been well

5:19:47 documented that the brick includes

5:19:48 massive surveillance efforts, including

5:19:51 surveillance of lawful political

5:19:53 activity protected by the first

5:19:54 amendment. And we know that the brick,

5:19:57 which was originally created as an

5:19:58 anti-terrorism tool, has really drifted

5:20:01 from that mission and collects

5:20:02 information on everyday people who are

5:20:04 not suspected of any criminal activity

5:20:07 through all sorts of surveillance tools,

5:20:09 license plate readers, etc. We know that

5:20:11 the brick has monitored social media of

5:20:14 um Occupy Boston activists, Black Lives

5:20:17 Matter activists, Muslims. We also know

5:20:20 that the brick disproportionately

5:20:22 collects and stores information on

5:20:24 black, latina, and Muslim communities.

5:20:27 So much so that nearly 98% of the people

5:20:30 in the database uh in the gang database

5:20:33 that is maintained by the brick or

5:20:34 people of color. So to the extent that

5:20:37 the brick facilitates information

5:20:38 sharing among law enforcement agencies,

5:20:41 we are confident that Brookline has all

5:20:44 sorts of collaborative relationships uh

5:20:46 with our neighboring cities and towns

5:20:48 well beyond the handful uh of cities and

5:20:51 and towns that belong to the brick and

5:20:53 that any kind of needed information

5:20:55 sharing can and does still happen

5:20:56 outside of the program. Um, but most

5:20:59 importantly, we know that information

5:21:00 shared through the brick can and has

5:21:02 been used uh by immigration, by the

5:21:05 Department of Homeland Security, an

5:21:06 agency that I think we can all agree uh

5:21:09 right now at least is marked by lawless,

5:21:12 unconstitutional, and violent behavior.

5:21:15 Um, and in fact, there's a DHS agent

5:21:17 that is located within the brick. Um,

5:21:19 and we know that this information

5:21:21 sharing has led to deportation that's

5:21:23 been shown. Um, now town policies and

5:21:26 stated values are that we're not going

5:21:28 to use town resources to facilitate

5:21:30 civil immigration enforcement, but

5:21:32 staying in the brick does just that. The

5:21:35 second aspect of the resolution I wanted

5:21:37 to speak to calls for limiting the

5:21:39 sharing of fingerprints uh with the

5:21:41 federal government. We know that

5:21:43 immigration civil arrest can happen

5:21:45 after an individual is fingerprinted by

5:21:48 the local police and that's regardless

5:21:50 of whether that person is ultimately

5:21:52 charged or whether that person is

5:21:54 ultimately found guilty. And those

5:21:56 prints are currently automatically

5:21:57 shared

5:21:59 um with the federal government,

5:22:00 including immigration. Um in fact, most

5:22:04 if not all of immigration arrests that

5:22:06 have taken place in Brooklyn in the past

5:22:07 year have happened as a result of this

5:22:10 fingerprint sharing. So though we have

5:22:13 policies that are meant to ensure

5:22:14 Brooklyn police doesn't assist civil

5:22:17 immigration enforcement, sharing

5:22:19 fingerprints in that way does just that.

5:22:22 And so because we can't control how

5:22:24 fingerprints are shared among federal

5:22:26 agencies uh once they they're passed on

5:22:29 to federal agencies, what we can control

5:22:31 is is when we share them. And so

5:22:33 limiting the sharing of prints uh for

5:22:36 children uh and for those with lower

5:22:39 level offenses would not only ensure

5:22:41 that we're acting in accordance with

5:22:43 their values, but would also allow due

5:22:45 process in criminal proceedings.

5:22:48 If someone who's arrested on a criminal

5:22:50 charge is then whisked away by ICE, the

5:22:54 criminal case remains open. That person

5:22:56 is not given an opportunity to defend

5:22:58 themselves.

5:22:59 That victim is not given an opportunity

5:23:01 for a resolution in that case. Um and

5:23:04 finally, it would help to avoid

5:23:06 disproportionate consequences, right?

5:23:08 Someone may be arrested by the police,

5:23:12 um, may even be found guilty or, you

5:23:15 know, receive a sentence or probation,

5:23:17 but a non-citizen charged with the same

5:23:20 offense would instead find themselves in

5:23:22 the hands of ICE and facing deportation.

5:23:24 And I've seen this play out time and

5:23:26 time again in my work, including in a

5:23:28 case that I was working with today, um,

5:23:31 and countless families torn apart as a

5:23:34 result of that desperate outcome.

5:23:37 And then the last topic uh we wanted to

5:23:39 address this evening is the UAC funding

5:23:42 uh which I know that the select board

5:23:44 discussed uh not too long ago. Um among

5:23:47 other things UAC funding supports our

5:23:50 participation in the brick but it's not

5:23:51 the only thing that it does. Um, and

5:23:55 specifically I wanted to point out that

5:23:57 the federal fiscal year 2025 notice of

5:24:00 funding opportunity, the NOFO put out by

5:24:02 the federal government for these grants

5:24:04 requires uh recipients to spend 30%

5:24:09 uh of the funds that are received on

5:24:11 what they identify as national priority

5:24:13 areas. And the boxes that you see here,

5:24:16 I apologize, I'm not sure why they're

5:24:17 showing up black with white print. Hope

5:24:20 they're not too hard to read. um are

5:24:22 screenshots, screen grabs directly from

5:24:24 from the NOFO and that includes a

5:24:28 minimum spend requirement for all

5:24:30 recipients on what they call supporting

5:24:33 border crisis response and enforcement.

5:24:36 Um and the types of activities that the

5:24:38 NOFO lists as examples of this are types

5:24:41 of activities that we've already said as

5:24:43 a town we're not going to do, right?

5:24:45 participation in 287g agreements,

5:24:48 cooperation with detainers, support of

5:24:51 enforcement of immigration law. Now, in

5:24:54 the proclamation that we've been

5:24:55 discussing for some time this evening,

5:24:57 um the select board stated, and I quote,

5:24:59 "The town of Brooklyn does not aid,

5:25:02 abet, or participate in any way in civil

5:25:06 immigration enforcement." And so, it's

5:25:08 clear that we can't apply for and

5:25:10 receive funding that has these types of

5:25:12 strings attached. Um, so I will end

5:25:15 there. Thank you very much.

5:25:17 Look forward to the questions.

5:25:20 Any questions before we go to public

5:25:22 hearing?

5:25:23 I do just want to note Chief Sullivan is

5:25:25 online. Um, Chief Sullivan administers

5:25:28 our um relationship with the consortium

5:25:32 on Yuasi. Um I will let him speak to

5:25:36 some of the issues here, but I will just

5:25:38 note that there's been an injunction uh

5:25:41 that wave that uh waves that requirement

5:25:44 that immigration that we that we spend

5:25:47 some of that money on immigration

5:25:48 enforcement. Um so that's a positive

5:25:50 development for us. Um

5:25:53 um and under those circumstances, UASI

5:25:57 remains an extremely critical source of

5:26:00 funding, especially given the funding

5:26:02 crisis that we're currently in. Um I

5:26:05 think it would be, you know, it would be

5:26:08 extremely difficult for us um to

5:26:12 um operate without those funds given the

5:26:14 current economic conditions that we're

5:26:16 in. Um but I'd let Chief Sullivan speak

5:26:18 on that matter. Um,

5:26:24 Chief Sullivan,

5:26:26 where are you? There you go.

5:26:31 Good evening. Uh, John Sullivan, uh,

5:26:33 fire chief and emergency management

5:26:35 director and also the jurisdictional

5:26:37 point of contact for the UASI um

5:26:40 metropolitan um homeland security

5:26:42 region. Um, yes, to Chaz's point, um,

5:26:46 when the NOFO came out for the fiscal 25

5:26:49 grant, uh, and we, we parsed through all

5:26:53 of the, uh, conditions, uh, I was

5:26:55 actually the one that brought this issue

5:26:58 up before all the JPOX at that at that

5:27:02 meeting. Um we we reached out through

5:27:05 EOPS uh the executive office of public

5:27:08 safety uh which administers the grant

5:27:10 for the state uh and um looked for

5:27:14 clarification on how that was going to

5:27:16 affect us. uh and that it was made clear

5:27:20 that not only Brookline but many of the

5:27:23 jurisdictions within the MBHSR

5:27:27 uh have substantial conflicts with the

5:27:31 requirements uh that were just noted by

5:27:34 the petitioners. um that was um put to

5:27:38 EOPS which was then put to FEMA. Uh, and

5:27:41 as Chaz just pointed out, um, the the um

5:27:45 the injunction was put forth in when we

5:27:48 we were just uh given a uh the just last

5:27:52 week the the go-ahad from EOPS to be

5:27:55 able to then um make allocations and

5:28:00 submit our final um um our final bid, if

5:28:05 you will, for the grant. Um, I would

5:28:08 just say that it was it was made clear

5:28:12 uh the position of Brookline uh and many

5:28:15 other communities with regard to these

5:28:17 provisions and u that it would be a

5:28:21 non-starter for us. Um, I think this

5:28:24 petition at this point uh goes um goes

5:28:29 against us in terms of our ability to um

5:28:33 continue to receive those grants without

5:28:38 those provisions. Um it would it would

5:28:41 it the way that I read it, it it

5:28:43 requires us to withdraw from UASI. Uh

5:28:46 and um as Chaz just pointed out, there

5:28:49 has been hundreds of thousands of

5:28:51 dollars over the years to not only the

5:28:53 police department, um the fire

5:28:56 department, the health department, the

5:28:58 IT department, uh for uh cyber security

5:29:02 initiatives, um the pays for a number of

5:29:05 the cyber security initiatives for that.

5:29:07 uh also pays for uh some wonderful

5:29:11 programs that we've had throughout the

5:29:13 years in Brooklyn uh like the uh the uh

5:29:16 EP Buddies program and the Tech Buddies

5:29:19 program which benefited our seniors

5:29:21 during COVID uh and allowed them to get

5:29:25 access to um email addresses and um

5:29:29 technologies that they would never have

5:29:30 had otherwise and training through um

5:29:34 the grant. uh it it it it provides us

5:29:37 with a myriad of things that go

5:29:41 nowhere near any of the the petitioners

5:29:45 um problematic areas. And so I would

5:29:48 just caution that, you know, we'd be

5:29:50 throwing the baby out with the

5:29:51 bathwater. There was there's there's I

5:29:54 think a better way for us to address the

5:29:58 concerns of the petitioners and and

5:30:00 uphold what we know as the um

5:30:05 representatives of this town at at this

5:30:08 level of government. Uh what what the

5:30:12 feelings of the town are and what the

5:30:14 this community represents. Um I I'm just

5:30:18 concerned that the way this is is uh is

5:30:21 put forth that it it doesn't give us an

5:30:24 ability to move forward and try to work

5:30:26 within the system while continuing to

5:30:28 uphold uh the values and the uh wishes

5:30:32 of the the greater uh community. So um I

5:30:36 would caution that

5:30:39 it's not it's not well thought through

5:30:42 that particular piece of this. I I I

5:30:44 can't address the other things about the

5:30:46 brick. Uh Paul Campbell is here to be

5:30:47 able to do that. Uh but uh this piece

5:30:51 about the uh the UASI funding um it it

5:30:54 it does it does give me pause and I I

5:30:58 would ask that we consider um removing

5:31:05 was to go forward.

5:31:07 Okay. Uh, I see Alex Leo's um, excuse

5:31:11 me, he has his hand hand up.

5:31:14 Yeah, thanks so much, Bernard. I I just

5:31:15 wanted to to respond briefly to uh, to

5:31:18 Chief Sullivan and first off, thank him

5:31:19 for um, his many years representing

5:31:22 Brooklyn uh, in this consortium. Um, and

5:31:25 you know, I think from our perspective,

5:31:27 um, this is part of what is so

5:31:37 conditioning to AASI funding is they are

5:32:26 has made both now we think a legal and

5:32:34 participate in federal funding

5:32:35 activities where that kind of
